- Injunction (interim/permanent), damages or account of profits, delivery-up of infringing goods, costs.

## 3.3 Worked KTU-Style Analytical Example

**Problem:** "ABC Pvt. Ltd. invents a low-cost water purifier for rural India in 2018, files a provisional on 01-01-2019, complete specification on 15-12-2019, and gets grant on 01-06-2022. A competitor XYZ starts selling an identical purifier in March 2021. Discuss the rights, timeline, and remedies."

**Analytical Walkthrough (as expected by KTU examiner):**

- **Priority Date:** 01-01-2019 (provisional filing date).
- **Publication Date:** 18 months from priority = **01-07-2020**.
- **Grant Date:** 01-06-2022.
- **Infringement Date:** March 2021 — *between publication and grant.*
- **Rights in March 2021:** ABC holds **"provisional rights"** under **Section 11A(7)** — can claim damages from the date of publication IF the infringer had actual notice.
- **Absolute Rights:** Enforceable from 01-06-2022.
- **Remedy:** ABC files a suit under **Section 104 of the Patents Act** in the District Court / High Court. Can claim injunction + damages + account of profits.
- **Term Expiry:** Patent expires on **01-01-2039** (20 years from filing). Renewal fees payable from 2022 onwards annually.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Patent Filing Procedure Flow (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inventor Conceives Invention] --> B[Prior Art Search via InPASS Google Patents]
    B --> C{Is Novelty Established?}
    C -->|No| D[Abandon or Modify Invention]
    C -->|Yes| E[Draft Provisional Specification]
    E --> F[File Patent Application Form 1 Form 2 Online at ipindiaonline]
    F --> G[Priority Date Recorded]
    G --> H[Publication in Patent Journal 18 Months]
    H --> I[Pre Grant Opposition Window 6 Months]
    I --> J[Applicant Files Request for Examination Form 18 within 48 Months]
    J --> K[Patent Examiner Issues First Examination Report]
    K --> L{Objections Overcome?}
    L -->|No| M[Hearing before Controller]
    M --> L
    L -->|Yes| N[Patent GRANTED]
    N --> O[Renewal Fees Form 4 Annually from Year 3]
    O --> P[20 Year Term Expiry]
    P --> Q[Enters Public Domain]
```

## 4.2 Patent Infringement Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Patent Granted with Claims] --> S[Identify Accused Product or Process]
    S --> T{Literal Infringement under All Elements Rule}
    T -->|Yes all elements match| Z[Infringement Established]
    T -->|No one element missing| U[Apply Doctrine of Equivalents FWR Test]
    U --> V{Substantially Same Function Way Result}
    V -->|Yes| Z
    V -->|No| W[Check Defenses Prior Use Invalidity Bolar]
    W --> X{Defense Valid}
    X -->|Yes| Y[No Infringement]
    X -->|No| Z
    Z --> AA[Seek Injunction Damages Account of Profits]
```

## 4.3 Types of Patent Applications (Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PA[Patent Application Types in India] --> P1[Ordinary Application]
    PA --> P2[Convention Application 12 Month Priority]
    PA --> P3[PCT National Phase Application 30 31 Month]
    PA --> P4[Provisional Specification 12 Months]
    PA --> P5[Complete Specification]
    PA --> P6[Divisional Application from Earlier Pending App]
    PA --> P7[Patent of Addition for Improvement]
```

## 4.4 Patentability Test (Three-Pillar Logic Gate)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Invention] --> B{Novel Section 2 l}
    B -->|No| X[REJECTED]
    B -->|Yes| C{Inventive Step Section 2 ja}
    C -->|No| X
    C -->|Yes| D{Industrial Application Section 2 ac}
    D -->|No| X
    D -->|Yes| E{Excluded Subject Matter Section 3 4}
    E -->|Yes| X
    E -->|No| F[PATENT GRANTED]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Patent. State the duration of patent protection in India.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A patent is a statutory intellectual property right granted by the government to an inventor for a new, useful, and non-obvious invention, conferring the exclusive right to make, use, sell, and import the invention for a limited period in exchange for public disclosure of its technical details. Under the Indian Patents Act, 1970 (amended 2005), the duration of a patent is **20 years** from the date of filing of the application, subject to the payment of annual renewal fees from the 3rd year onwards.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Provisional Specification and Complete Specification.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Provisional Specification | Complete Specification |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Establishes priority date | Full technical disclosure with claims |
| **Details** | Brief description only | Title, background, detailed description, claims, abstract |
| **Time Limit** | Must file complete spec within 12 months | No such limit (after filing) |
| **Rights** | No enforceable rights yet | Forms basis of granted patent |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the essential criteria for patentability under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. Discuss with examples what is not patentable in India.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three essential cumulative criteria for patentability are:

1. **Novelty (Section 2(1)(l)):** The invention must be *new*. India follows the **worldwide novelty standard** — any prior public disclosure, anywhere in the world, before the priority date destroys novelty. *Example:* A thesis published in Japan in 2015 would destroy novelty of an identical Indian application filed in 2017.

2. **Inventive Step / Non-Obviousness (Section 2(1)(ja)):** The invention must involve a technical feature that is *not obvious* to a person skilled in the art (PHOSITA). *Example:* Replacing a metal screw with a plastic screw for corrosion resistance in marine applications may be obvious, but replacing it with a biodegradable polymer may constitute an inventive step.

3. **Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac)):** The invention must be capable of being made or used in an industry. Purely theoretical scientific discoveries fail this test.

**What is NOT patentable (Section 3 & 4):**
- Frivolous inventions, those contrary to public order or morality (Section 3(b)).
- Scientific principles, abstract ideas, mathematical or business methods (Section 3(c)).
- Mere discovery of substances found in nature (Section 3(c)).
- New use of a known substance without forming a new product.
- Plants, animals, and essentially biological processes for production (Section 3(j)) — *but transgenic plants and micro-organisms ARE patentable.*
- Computer programs *per se* — *but software embedded in a technical invention IS patentable.*
- Atomic energy inventions (Section 4).

**(b) Describe the step-by-step procedure for filing and granting a patent in India. Mention the role of Form 18.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Invention Disclosure & Prior Art Search:** Inventor searches InPASS, Google Patents, Espacenet for prior art.
2. **Drafting of Specification:** Either Provisional (for priority date) or Complete Specification (Form 2).
3. **Filing of Application:** Online filing via IP India portal using Form 1 (Application for Grant), Form 2 (Specification), Form 3 (Statement of Foreign Filings), and Form 5 (Declaration of Inventorship).
4. **Publication:** Application is published **18 months** after priority date in the Patent Office Journal. Pre-grant opposition window opens.
5. **Request for Examination (Form 18):** The applicant must file **Form 18 — Request for Examination** within **48 months** of the priority date. This is a critical step; without Form 18, the application is deemed abandoned. Form 18 triggers the patent examiner to begin substantive examination.
6. **Examination & FER:** The examiner issues a First Examination Report (FER) with objections. Applicant has 6 months (extendable) to respond via Form 7 (Amendments) or Form 8 (Statement).
7. **Hearing:** If objections persist, a hearing is held before the Controller of Patents.
8. **Grant:** Once accepted, the patent is granted, entered in the Register of Patents, and published in the Patent Office Journal.
9. **Renewal:** Annual renewal fees (Form 4) from 3rd year onwards for the full 20-year term.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) What is patent infringement? Explain the Doctrine of Equivalents with the FWR Test.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Patent Infringement** is the unauthorized making, using, selling, offering for sale, or importing of a patented invention within the territory of India during the patent term, without the patentee's license.

**Two Doctrines of Infringement:**

1. **Literal Infringement:** When every element of a patent claim is found exactly in the accused product/process (All-Elements Rule).
2. **Doctrine of Equivalents (DoE):** Applied when literal infringement fails. The accused product may still infringe if it performs the **same function**, in the **same way**, to achieve the **same result** as the patented invention, even if structurally different.

**FWR Test (Function – Way – Result Test):**
- **Function:** Does the equivalent element perform substantially the *same function* in the invention?
- **Way:** Does it do so in substantially the *same way*?
- **Result:** Does it achieve substantially the *same result*?

If the answer to all three is *yes*, infringement is established under DoE. This prevents infringers from escaping liability by making *minor insubstantial changes* to the patented invention.

**(b) Discuss the rights of a patentee in India. What are the remedies available for patent infringement?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Rights of a Patentee (Section 48 of the Patents Act, 1970):**
A patentee has the exclusive right to:
1. Make the patented product or use the patented process.
2. Sell, offer for sale, or distribute the patented product.
3. Import the patented product or product obtained by the patented process.
4. Stock the product for the purpose of offering it for sale.
5. **Right to assign, license, or transfer** the patent (Section 69).
6. **Right to claim damages** for infringement (Section 108).
7. Right to file a **PCT application** if Indian national/resident (Section 133).
8. **Right to seek compulsory license** if reasonable requirements of public are not met (Section 84).

**Remedies for Infringement (Sections 104–114):**

1. **Injunction:** Interim (during trial) or permanent (after trial) — the most potent remedy.
2. **Damages or Account of Profits:** The patentee can elect between *damages* (compensation for loss) or *account of profits* (infringer's ill-gotten gains).
3. **Delivery-up or Destruction:** Court can order the infringing goods to be destroyed, forfeited, or delivered to the patentee.
4. **Costs:** Reasonable litigation costs awarded to the successful party.
5. **Anton Piller Order** (in England; in India, analogous civil search orders) — pre-trial search of defendant's premises.
6. **Certificate of Validity:** If the court upholds the patent, a certificate is issued, which acts as estoppel against any future infringer.
7. **Criminal Prosecution** under the Copyright Act (not Patents Act — note this KTU distinction).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU 2024 Patents Questions:**
> 1. Writing "patent rights begin from filing date" — **WRONG.** Rights begin from **date of GRANT** (Section 53). Section 11A(7) gives only *provisional* interim rights from publication.
> 2. Confusing *provisional* with *complete specification* — Provisional is NOT a patent; it is only a priority date placeholder.
> 3. Stating "patents are valid worldwide" — **WRONG.** Patents are *territorial*. WIPO/PCT does not grant a global patent; it only streamlines filing.
> 4. Saying "computer programs are patentable in India" — **PARTIALLY WRONG.** Software *per se* is not patentable; only software with a *technical effect* or *technical contribution* is.
> 5. Confusing **Patent Term (20 years)** with **Copyright Term (60 years)** — these are distinct IP regimes.
> 6. Forgetting **Section 3(j)** — plants and animals are NOT patentable, but micro-organisms and GMOs ARE.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Patents**

- ✅ **Definition:** Statutory monopoly for a technical invention, granted in exchange for public disclosure.
- ✅ **Term:** **20 years** from filing date; renewable annually from Year 3.
- ✅ **Territorial:** India only; for global protection, file PCT (within 12 months of first filing).
- ✅ **Three Patentability Criteria:** **Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application** (all three mandatory).
- ✅ **Novelty Standard:** **Worldwide / Absolute Novelty** (India follows global standard since 2005 amendment).
- ✅ **Not Patentable:** Abstract ideas, business methods, software *per se*, plants/animals, atomic energy, mere discoveries, traditional knowledge.
- ✅ **Patentable:** Micro-organisms, GM crops, software with technical effect, new chemical entity, new mechanical device, new process.
- ✅ **Specifications:** Provisional (priority date, 12 months) + Complete (claims, full disclosure).
- ✅ **Form 18 — Request for Examination:** Mandatory within **48 months** of priority date.
- ✅ **Publication:** Automatic at **18 months** after priority date.
- ✅ **Rights begin:** From **date of GRANT** (not filing or publication).
- ✅ **Infringement Doctrines:** Literal (All-Elements) + Doctrine of Equivalents (**FWR Test**).
- ✅ **Remedies:** Injunction, damages, account of profits, delivery-up of infringing goods.
- ✅ **Statute:** Patents Act, 1970 (amended 2005); governed by CGPDTM, DPIIT.
- ✅ **Key International Conventions:** Paris Convention (priority), PCT (international filing), TRIPS (WTO global IP standards), Budapest Treaty (micro-organism deposit).
- ✅ **Startup Benefit:** Under SIPP scheme, startups get **80% rebate** on patent filing fees.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trademarks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT633 - INNOVATION & ENTREPRENEURSHIP > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Trademarks # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Trademark** is a distinctive sign, symbol, word, phrase, logo, design, or combination thereof that is used to identify and distinguish the goods or services of one enterprise from those of others in the marketplace. It serves as a unique identifier of the **commercial source** of products or services and is a critical component of **Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)**. In the Indian legal context, trademarks are governed by the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** (which replaced the earlier Trade and Merchandise Marks Act, 1958). The Act defines a trademark under Section 2(1)(zb) as: > *"a mark capable of being represented graphically and which is capable of distinguishing the goods or services of one person from those of others and may include a brand name, trademark, label, ticket, name, signature, word, letter, numeral, shape of goods, packaging or combination of colours or any combination thereof."* A trademark can be registered under the **Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks**, which operates under the **Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT)**, Ministry of Commerce and Industry, Government of India. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:** Trademarks form part of the broader module on **Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)**, which is essential for innovation and entrepreneurship as it protects brand equity, fosters fair competition, and enables business valuation. > [!NOTE] > **Key Symbol of Registration:** In India, the registered trademark is denoted by the **®** symbol. An unregistered trademark can use the **™** symbol (or **℠** for services) to indicate a claim of ownership. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you walk into a crowded marketplace where every vendor is selling similar products — rice, spices, or mobile phones. How do you instantly recognize *your* preferred brand? It's the **logo on the package**, the **distinctive name**, the **unique color combination**, or the **signature tagline**. That recognizable identity is the **trademark**. Think of a trademark like a **student's roll number in an examination hall**: - Your roll number **uniquely identifies you** among thousands of students. - No two students can have the same roll number (well, they shouldn't!). - It allows the examiner to **attribute your answer sheet to you** (source identification). - If someone copies your roll number to submit their work as yours, that's **infringement**. Similarly, a trademark: - **Uniquely identifies** the source of a product or service. - **Prevents competitors** from using the same or confusingly similar marks. - **Builds brand reputation** over time through consistent quality. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Example:** The **"NIKE" swoosh logo** and the **"Just Do It" tagline** are registered trademarks. When you see them, you immediately associate the product with Nike Inc. — this is the power of a trademark in establishing **brand equity**. ## 1.3 Essential Characteristics of a Valid Trademark For a mark to be registrable as a trademark, it must satisfy the following criteria: | **Characteristic** | **Description** | |---|---| | **Distinctiveness** | Must be capable of distinguishing the goods/services of one trader from another. Generic or descriptive marks (e.g., "Apple" for apples) are usually refused. | | **Graphical Representation** | Must be representable in a visual format (under the 1999 Act, this requirement has been relaxed to include sound, smell, etc., with the 2017 amendments). | | **Non-Descriptive** | Should not describe the quality, character, or nature of the goods directly. | | **Not Deceptive** | Must not be likely to deceive or cause confusion. | | **Not Prohibited** | Should not contain prohibited content (national flags, state emblems, names of saints, etc., under Section 13). | | **Use in Trade** | Must be used or proposed to be used in the course of trade. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Slogan to Remember for KTU Exams:** *"Distinctive, Representable, Non-Descriptive, Non-Deceptive — the four pillars of a valid trademark."* # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Trademarks Trademarks can be broadly classified into the following categories based on their nature and registration status: ### A. Based on Type of Mark 1. **Word Mark / Wordmark:** A trademark consisting of text only (e.g., "GOOGLE," "AMAZON," "TATA"). Offers the broadest legal protection. 2. **Device Mark / Logo:** A graphical representation, symbol, or design (e.g., the Apple logo, the Nike swoosh). 3. **Composite Mark:** A combination of word and device (e.g., "Coca-Cola" written in its signature Spencerian script). 4. **Three-Dimensional (3D) Mark:** Shape of the product or its packaging (e.g., the shape of a Coca-Cola bottle, the Toblerone chocolate bar's triangular prism). 5. **Sound Mark:** An aural signature (e.g., the Nokia tune, the MGM lion's roar). Recognized in India after the 2017 amendment to the Trade Marks Rules. 6. **Colour Mark:** A single colour or combination of colours used as a brand identifier (e.g., the specific shade of magenta used by T-Mobile). 7. **Slogan Mark:** A catchy phrase used to promote a brand (e.g., "Just Do It" by Nike, "I'm Lovin' It" by McDonald's). 8. **Certification Mark:** A mark indicating that goods/services meet certain standards (e.g., the ISI mark by Bureau of Indian Standards, the Woolmark). 9. **Collective Mark:** Used by a group of traders to indicate membership (e.g., "Chanderi" sarees by a weavers' cooperative). ### B. Based on Registration Status 1. **Registered Trademark (®):** Officially registered with the Trade Marks Registry. Offers stronger legal protection, including the right to sue for infringement and claim damages. 2. **Unregistered Trademark (™):** Used in trade but not officially registered. Offers limited protection under **passing off** (a common law remedy). ## 2.2 The Nice Classification System Internationally, trademarks are classified according to the **Nice Classification** (NCL), established by the **Nice Agreement (1957)**. It categorizes goods and services into **45 classes**: - **Classes 1–34:** Goods (e.g., Class 25 for clothing, Class 9 for electronics) - **Classes 35–45:** Services (e.g., Class 41 for education, Class 42 for software services) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Nice Classification is administered by the **World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO)**, Geneva. When filing a trademark application in India, the applicant must specify the class(es) under which the mark is to be registered. ## 2.3 Rights Conferred by Trademark Registration Under the **Trade Marks Act, 1999 (Sections 28 and 29)**, registration confers the following **exclusive rights** on the proprietor: | **Right** | **Description** | |---|---| | **Right to Use** | Exclusive right to use the mark in relation to the goods/services for which it is registered. | | **Right to Assign** | Right to assign or transfer the trademark to another party. | | **Right to License** | Right to grant licenses to others for use. | | **Right to Sue for Infringement** | Right to file a civil suit and claim damages for unauthorized use. | | **Right to Seek Criminal Action** | Under Section 103, criminal prosecution for selling goods with false trademarks (imprisonment up to 3 years or fine, or both). | | **Right to Customs Protection** | Right to record the trademark with Indian Customs to prevent import of counterfeit goods. | ## 2.4 Grounds for Refusal of Registration (Section 9 & 11) The Registrar can refuse registration on the following grounds: - **Absolute Grounds (Section 9):** - Marks that are not distinctive. - Marks that consist exclusively of descriptive terms. - Marks that have become customary in the trade. - Marks that consist of shapes resulting from the nature of goods, technical necessity, or that give substantial value. - Marks that are contrary to public order or morality. - **Relative Grounds (Section 11):** - Likelihood of confusion with earlier trademarks. - Identity or similarity to well-known trademarks. - Use of the mark would take unfair advantage of, or be detrimental to, the distinctive character or repute of an earlier mark. ## 2.5 Duration, Renewal & Infringement | **Aspect** | **KTU 2024 High-Yield Detail** | |---|---| | **Initial Registration Term** | **10 years** from the date of filing the application. | | **Renewal** | Can be renewed indefinitely for successive periods of **10 years** each by filing Form TM-R within 6 months before expiry. | | **Infringement** | Unauthorized use of a registered trademark that causes confusion. Remedies include injunction, damages, account of profits, and delivery up of infringing goods. | | **Passing Off** | Common law remedy for unregistered trademarks. Plaintiff must prove **reputation, misrepresentation, and damage**. | | **Well-Known Marks** | Marks recognized by the public at large (e.g., Coca-Cola, Google). Receive higher protection even if not registered in India. | ## 2.6 Real-World Utility for Engineers & Entrepreneurs For B.Tech students, understanding trademarks is critical for: 1. **Startup Branding:** Choosing a distinctive, registrable name and logo. 2. **Brand Valuation:** Trademarks contribute significantly to a company's **intangible asset value**. 3. **Licensing Revenue:** Franchising models (e.g., McDonald's) rely entirely on trademark licensing. 4. **Competitive Intelligence:** Avoiding infringement when launching new products. 5. **Global Expansion:** Trademark protection under the **Madrid Protocol** allows international registration in 130+ countries via a single application. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Link:** This topic maps to **CO1 (Understand the fundamentals of IPR and innovation)** and **CO2 (Apply IP concepts to entrepreneurial ventures)**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Process Analysis & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Trademark Registration Process in India (Step-by-Step) The trademark registration process in India follows a structured sequence governed by the **Trade Marks Rules, 2017** (as amended). Below is the exhaustive, step-by-step analysis: ### **Step 1: Trademark Search** Before filing, conduct a search in the **Trade Marks Registry database** (available at the official IP India portal) to ensure no identical or similar mark exists in the same class. > **Purpose:** To avoid rejection and opposition at later stages. ### **Step 2: Filing of Application (Form TM-A)** The applicant files **Form TM-A** either: - **Online** (preferred) via the IP India e-filing portal, or - **Offline** at the appropriate Trade Marks Registry office (Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai, or Ahmedabad based on jurisdiction). **Fee Structure (as per Schedule I, Trade Marks Rules, 2017):** | **Entity Type** | **Fee (per class) — e-filing** | |---|---| | Individual / Startup / Small Enterprise | ₹ 4,500 | | Others (Companies, LLPs, etc.) | ₹ 9,000 | > **Key Inputs Required:** Applicant's name and address, representation of the mark, list of goods/services with class numbers, date of first use (if any), user affidavit. ### **Step 3: Vienna Codification** For device marks, the **Vienna Classification** (administered by WIPO) is used to classify the figurative elements. The Registry assigns a Vienna code for administrative purposes. ### **Step 4: Examination by the Registrar** The Examiner reviews the application for compliance with: - **Section 9** (Absolute grounds for refusal) - **Section 11** (Relative grounds — conflict with prior marks) - **Section 13** (Prohibited marks) The Examiner issues an **Examination Report** within **3 months** (as per 2017 Rules). ### **Step 5: Response to Examination Report** The applicant must respond within **30 days** (extendable by 1 month) addressing the objections raised. ### **Step 6: Publication in the Trade Marks Journal** If accepted, the mark is **published in the Trade Marks Journal** for **4 months** (opposition period). During this period, any third party can file **Notice of Opposition (Form TM-O)**. ### **Step 7: Opposition Proceedings (if any)** If opposition is filed: - Both parties submit evidence (counter-statement, evidence in support, evidence in reply). - The Registrar conducts a hearing and issues a **decision**. ### **Step 8: Registration & Issuance of Certificate** If no opposition is filed, or if opposition is decided in favour of the applicant, the mark is **registered**, and a **Registration Certificate** is issued with the **®** symbol. ### **Step 9: Renewal** Trademark is valid for **10 years** from the filing date. Renewal can be filed within 6 months before expiry (Form TM-R). ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Types of Trademarks vs. Examples | **Type of Mark** | **Legal Basis** | **Real-World Example** | **KTU Exam Tip** | |---|---|---|---| | Word Mark | Sec. 2(1)(zb), Trade Marks Act, 1999 | "TATA," "INFOSYS" | Most common type; broadest protection. | | Device Mark | Sec. 2(1)(zb), Trade Marks Act, 1999 | Apple logo, Twitter bird | Must file graphical representation. | | Sound Mark | Rule 2(k), TM Rules, 2017 | Nokia Tune, MGM Lion Roar | Recognized post-2017 amendment. | | 3D Shape Mark | Sec. 2(1)(zb), Trade Marks Act, 1999 | Coca-Cola bottle, KitKat bar | Cannot be a shape that gives substantial value. | | Colour Mark | Sec. 2(1)(zb), Trade Marks Act, 1999 | T-Mobile Magenta | Must show acquired distinctiveness. | | Slogan Mark | Sec. 2(1)(zb), Trade Marks Act, 1999 | "Just Do It" (Nike) | Must be distinctive, not merely descriptive. | | Certification Mark | Sec. 2(1)(d), Trade Marks Act, 1999 | ISI Mark, AGMARK | Indicates quality standards. | | Collective Mark | Sec. 2(1)(g), Trade Marks Act, 1999 | "Swiss Made" watches, "Chanderi" sarees | Used by association of traders. | ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Trademark vs. Other IP Rights | **Parameter** | **Trademark** | **Patent** | **Copyright** | **Design** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **What it Protects** | Brand identity (name, logo, slogan) | Inventions & technical innovations | Literary, artistic, musical works | Aesthetic appearance of an article | | **Governing Law** | Trade Marks Act, 1999 | Patents Act, 1970 | Copyright Act, 1957 | Designs Act, 2000 | | **Duration** | 10 years (renewable indefinitely) | 20 years from filing | Life + 60 years (literary); varies for others | 10 years (extendable by 5 years, max 15) | | **Registration Required?** | No (but recommended) | Yes (mandatory) | No (automatic upon creation) | Yes (mandatory) | | **Symbol** | ® / ™ | "Patented" or Pat. No. | © | "Regd." or "D" | | **Example** | "NIKE" wordmark | iPhone's slide-to-unlock mechanism | "Harry Potter" novel | Curved shape of a Coca-Cola bottle (aesthetic) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** In Part A questions, students often confuse trademark with copyright. Remember: **Copyright protects expression (the work itself), while trademark protects the source identifier (the brand).** A logo can be BOTH a copyright (artistic work) and a trademark (source identifier) — but the protections are distinct. ## 3.4 Analytical Framework: Likelihood of Confusion Test When determining whether a mark infringes another, courts apply the **"Likelihood of Confusion"** test. The key factors (derived from global jurisprudence and Indian case law) are: $$ \text{Confusion Score} = f(\text{Similarity of Marks}, \text{Similarity of Goods}, \text{Strength of Mark}, \text{Actual Confusion}, \text{Marketing Channels}) $$ While no single formula gives a numerical answer, Indian courts (following the **Cadila v. Cadila Pharmaceuticals, 2001** precedent) consider the following **6 parameters**: 1. **Similarity of the marks** (sight, sound, meaning). 2. **Similarity of the goods/services.** 3. **Class of purchasers** (general public vs. specialized). 4. **Nature of the market** (mass market vs. niche). 5. **Degree of reputation** of the senior mark. 6. **Evidence of actual confusion** in trade. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** For 7-mark sub-questions, always cite **at least one case law** (e.g., *Cadila v. Cadila Pharmaceuticals*, *Satyam Infoway v. Siffynet Solutions*) along with the statutory provision to earn full marks. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Trademark Registration Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Applicant Initiates TM Search

It is governed by the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** in India. **Two Essential Characteristics:** 1. **Distinctiveness (1.5 marks):** The mark must be capable of distinguishing the goods/services of one trader from another. A generic or purely descriptive term (e.g., "Cold" for ice cream) is not inherently distinctive unless it has acquired secondary meaning through prolonged use. 2. **Graphical Representation (1.5 marks):** As per Section 2(1)(zb) of the Trade Marks Act, 1999, the mark must be capable of being represented graphically. The 2017 amendment has expanded this to include non-visual marks like sounds, provided they can be represented in written form (e.g., musical notation). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only the definition and forget to explain the characteristics. Both parts are mandatory for full 3 marks. Also, do **not** confuse "trademark" with "brand" — a brand is the broader concept, while a trademark is its legally protectable element. --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** **Differentiate between a Registered Trademark and an Unregistered Trademark.** *`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | **Parameter** | **Registered Trademark (®)** | **Unregistered Trademark (™)** | |---|---|---| | **Legal Status** | Officially registered with the Trade Marks Registry under the Trade Marks Act, 1999. | Used in trade but not registered. | | **Rights Conferred** | Exclusive right to use, assign, license, and sue for infringement (Section 28). | Only common law remedy of **passing off** is available. | | **Protection** | Strong statutory protection across India for 10 years (renewable). | Limited to geographic area of actual use and reputation. | | **Symbol Used** | ® (registered) | ™ (claim of ownership) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Avoid one-line answers. Use a comparison table or bullet points to present contrasts clearly — this is a 3-mark "compare and contrast" question, not a definitional one. Writing only the symbol difference (® vs. ™) is **insufficient for full marks**. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **Discuss in detail the procedure for registration of a trademark in India under the Trade Marks Act, 1999. Highlight the grounds for refusal and the rights conferred upon registration.** *`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`* #### **Part (a) — Trademark Registration Procedure in India (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The registration of a trademark in India is governed by the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** and the **Trade Marks Rules, 2017**. The step-by-step procedure is as follows: **Step 1: Trademark Search (0.5 mark)** The applicant first conducts a search in the IP India database to ensure the proposed mark is not already registered or pending in the same class. This step is advisory but crucial to avoid objections later. **Step 2: Filing of Application — Form TM-A (1 mark)** The applicant files **Form TM-A** online (preferred) via the IP India portal or offline at the appropriate Trade Marks Registry office (Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai, or Ahmedabad). The application must include: - Applicant's name, address, and nationality. - Representation of the mark (text, logo, or both). - List of goods/services with **Nice Classification** class numbers. - Date of first use (if any) along with user affidavit. - Prescribed fee (₹4,500 for individuals/startups; ₹9,000 for others per class for e-filing). **Step 3: Vienna Codification (0.5 mark)** For device/figurative marks, the Registry assigns a **Vienna Classification** code to categorize the visual elements (administered by WIPO). **Step 4: Examination (1.5 marks)** The **Registrar of Trademarks** examines the application for compliance with: - **Section 9** — Absolute grounds (distinctiveness, descriptiveness, public order). - **Section 11** — Relative grounds (likelihood of confusion with prior marks, well-known marks). - **Section 13** — Prohibited marks (national symbols, emblems, etc.). The **Examination Report** is typically issued within **3 months** of filing. **Step 5: Response to Examination Report (0.5 mark)** The applicant must respond to any objections within **30 days** (extendable by 1 month), providing evidence and arguments to overcome the objections. **Step 6: Publication in Trade Marks Journal (1 mark)** If accepted, the mark is published in the **Trade Marks Journal** for a period of **4 months**. During this time, any third party can file a **Notice of Opposition (Form TM-O)**. **Step 7: Opposition Proceedings (0.5 mark)** If opposition is filed, both parties submit counter-statements, evidence, and the Registrar conducts a hearing before deciding. **Step 8: Registration (1 mark)** If no opposition is filed, or if opposition is decided in favour of the applicant, the mark is **registered**, and a **Registration Certificate** is issued. The applicant can now use the **®** symbol. The trademark is valid for **10 years** from the filing date. **Step 9: Renewal (0.5 mark)** Renewal can be filed in **Form TM-R** within 6 months before expiry, and is valid for another 10 years, indefinitely. #### **Part (b) — Grounds for Refusal & Rights Conferred (7 Marks)** **Grounds for Refusal of Registration (3.5 Marks):** **A. Absolute Grounds — Section 9 (2 marks):** - Marks that are **devoid of any distinctive character** (e.g., generic terms like "Premium" for quality goods). - Marks that consist exclusively of **descriptive terms** indicating the kind, quality, quantity, intended purpose, or other characteristics of goods. - Marks that have become **customary in the current language** or established trade practices. - Marks consisting of **shapes** that result from the nature of goods, are necessary to obtain a technical result, or give substantial value to the goods. - Marks that are **contrary to public order or morality**, or that may **deceive the public**. **B. Relative Grounds — Section 11 (1.5 marks):** - Identity or similarity to an **earlier trademark** causing likelihood of confusion. - Identity or similarity to a **well-known trademark** in India, even if registered for dissimilar goods, where use would dilute or take unfair advantage of its reputation. - Use of the mark would be prevented by **passing off** of the senior user's goods/services. **Rights Conferred by Registration (3.5 Marks):** Under **Section 28** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999, registration confers the following exclusive rights on the proprietor: 1. **Right to Exclusive Use (1 mark):** The registered proprietor has the exclusive right to use the trademark in relation to the goods/services for which it is registered. 2. **Right to Sue for Infringement (1 mark):** The proprietor can file a civil suit for infringement under Section 29, seeking injunction, damages, account of profits, and delivery up of infringing goods. 3. **Right to Assign and License (0.5 mark):** The proprietor can assign the trademark or grant licenses to others for use, including through franchising arrangements. 4. **Right to Criminal Action (0.5 mark):** Under **Section 103**, criminal prosecution can be initiated for applying false trademarks or selling goods with false marks, with penalties of imprisonment up to 3 years or fine, or both. 5. **Right to Customs Protection (0.5 mark):** The proprietor can record the trademark with **Indian Customs** to prevent import of counterfeit goods, leading to seizure at the border. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Common Mark Loss #1:** Forgetting to mention **Section 9 vs. Section 11** distinction (absolute vs. relative grounds) — this alone can cost **1.5 marks**. > - **Common Mark Loss #2:** Listing "rights" but not citing the **specific Section number** (e.g., Section 28 for exclusive rights, Section 103 for criminal action). Always cite statutory provisions. > - **Common Mark Loss #3:** Writing the procedure in a single paragraph. Use **numbered steps** for clarity and easy valuation. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** **Explain the various types of trademarks recognized under the Trade Marks Act, 1999, with suitable examples. Also, discuss the concept of infringement and the remedies available to the registered proprietor.** *`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`* #### **Part (a) — Types of Trademarks with Examples (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The **Trade Marks Act, 1999** recognizes various types of trademarks under its broad definition in **Section 2(1)(zb)**. The main types are: **1. Word Mark (1 mark):** A trademark consisting of text, letters, or numerals alone. - **Examples:** "TATA," "INFOSYS," "AMAZON," "SAMSUNG." - **Significance:** Offers the broadest legal protection as it covers the textual element irrespective of font, color, or styling. **2. Device Mark / Logo (1 mark):** A graphical or figurative element used as a trademark. - **Examples:** The Apple logo (a bitten apple), the Twitter bird, the Mercedes-Benz three-pointed star. - **Requirement:** Must be filed with a graphical representation. **3. Composite Mark (0.5 mark):** A combination of word and device elements. - **Examples:** "Coca-Cola" written in its signature Spencerian script, "Pepsi" with the red, white, and blue circle. **4. Three-Dimensional (3D) Shape Mark (1 mark):** The shape of the product or its packaging. - **Examples:** The triangular prism of a **Toblerone** chocolate bar, the curved shape of a **Coca-Cola** bottle, the shape of the **Ferrari** car grille. - **Limitation:** Cannot be a shape that gives substantial value to the goods or is technically necessary. **5. Sound Mark (1 mark):** An aural signature recognized as a trademark post the 2017 amendment to the Trade Marks Rules. - **Examples:** The **Nokia** tune, the **MGM** lion's roar, the **Intel** chime. - **Requirement:** Must be represented in a written format (e.g., musical notation) and clearly indicate the sound. **6. Colour Mark (0.5 mark):** A single colour or combination of colours used as a brand identifier. - **Examples:** The specific shade of **magenta** used by T-Mobile, the **purple** of Cadbury chocolate. - **Requirement:** Must demonstrate acquired distinctiveness through long and extensive use. **7. Slogan Mark (0.5 mark):** A catchy phrase used to promote a brand. - **Examples:** "Just Do It" (Nike), "I'm Lovin' It" (McDonald's), "The King of Good Times" (Kingfisher). - **Limitation:** Must be distinctive and not merely descriptive. **8. Certification Mark (0.5 mark):** Indicates that goods/services meet certain quality standards. - **Examples:** The **ISI** mark by the Bureau of Indian Standards, the **AGMARK** for agricultural products, the **Woolmark** for pure wool. **9. Collective Mark (0.5 mark):** Used by a group or association of traders to indicate membership. - **Examples:** **"Chanderi"** sarees by a weavers' cooperative, **"Swiss Made"** watches. **10. Series Mark (0.5 mark):** A family of marks that share a common element but differ in stylization or other features. - **Examples:** A cosmetic company registering "LUXE-1," "LUXE-2," "LUXE-3" as a series. #### **Part (b) — Trademark Infringement and Remedies (7 Marks)** **Concept of Infringement (3 Marks):** Trademark infringement is governed by **Section 29** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. A registered trademark is said to be infringed when an unauthorized person uses a mark that is **identical or deceptively similar** to the registered mark in relation to the same or similar goods/services, causing **likelihood of confusion or deception** among the public. **Key Tests for Infringement:** 1. **Likelihood of Confusion Test (1 mark):** The test established in *Cadila Pharmaceuticals Ltd. v. Cadila Healthcare Ltd. (2001)* laid down **6 factors**: - Nature of the marks (sight, sound, meaning). - Degree of resemblance between the marks. - Nature of the goods. - Class of purchasers (general public vs. specialized). - Manner of purchase (careful vs. casual). - Evidence of actual confusion. 2. **Well-Known Marks Protection (1 mark):** Under **Section 11(2) and (3)**, well-known marks receive **higher protection** even if not registered in India, especially when the use is detrimental to their distinctive character or repute. - **Example:** Marks like **Coca-Cola, Google, Microsoft** are recognized as well-known. 3. **Passing Off (1 mark):** For unregistered trademarks, the common law remedy of **passing off** is available, requiring the plaintiff to prove **reputation, misrepresentation, and damage**. - **Case Law:** *Reckitt & Colman Products Ltd. v. Borden Inc. (1990)* established the three-limb test of goodwill, misrepresentation, and damage. **Remedies Available (4 Marks):** | **Remedy** | **Description** | **Statutory Basis** | |---|---|---| | **Civil Remedies (2 marks)** | | | | Injunction | Restraining order to prevent further use of the infringing mark (interim or permanent). | Section 135 | | Damages | Monetary compensation for losses suffered. | Section 135 | | Account of Profits | The infringer must surrender profits earned from the infringement. | Section 135 | | Delivery Up | Court orders destruction of infringing goods, labels, and materials. | Section 135 | | **Criminal Remedies (1 mark)** | | | | Imprisonment | Up to **3 years** (extendable to 6 years for repeat offences). | Section 103 | | Fine | Monetary penalty. | Section 103 | | Seizure of Goods | Confiscation of counterfeit goods. | Section 104 | | **Administrative Remedies (1 mark)** | | | | Customs Recordal | Recording TM with Indian Customs to prevent import of counterfeits. | Customs Act, 1962 | | Domain Name Dispute (UDRP) | For online infringement, dispute can be resolved under ICANN's Uniform Domain-Name Dispute-Resolution Policy. | UDRP Rules | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Common Mark Loss #1:** Confusing **infringement (statutory remedy under Section 29)** with **passing off (common law remedy)**. They are distinct — infringement requires registration, while passing off is available even without registration. > - **Common Mark Loss #2:** Not citing the **Cadila case (2001)** for the likelihood of confusion test. Case law citation is **mandatory** for 7-mark sub-questions. > - **Common Mark Loss #3:** Listing only civil remedies and forgetting **criminal and administrative remedies**. The question asks for "remedies" in general, so cover all three categories. > - **Common Mark Loss #4:** Writing examples without explaining **why** they qualify as a particular type of trademark. Always link the example to the definition. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exams:** ### **Core Definitions** - ☐ **Trademark:** A distinctive sign identifying the source of goods/services — governed by the **Trade Marks Act, 1999**. - ☐ **® Symbol:** Used only for **registered** trademarks. - ☐ **™ Symbol:** Used for **unregistered** trademarks to assert a claim of ownership. - ☐ **Service Mark (℠):** Identifies a service rather than a product (treated similarly to trademarks under the 1999 Act). ### **Statutory Provisions to Memorize** - ☐ **Section 2(1)(zb):** Definition of trademark. - ☐ **Section 9:** Absolute grounds for refusal. - ☐ **Section 11:** Relative grounds for refusal (likelihood of confusion). - ☐ **Section 13:** Prohibited marks. - ☐ **Section 18:** Application for registration. - ☐ **Section 28:** Rights conferred by registration. - ☐ **Section 29:** Infringement of trademarks. - ☐ **Section 103:** Penalty for false trademark application. - ☐ **Section 135:** Civil remedies for infringement. ### **Numerical Values for the Exam** - ☐ Registration validity: **10 years** from filing date. - ☐ Renewal period: **10 years** (indefinite renewals). - ☐ Opposition period after Journal publication: **4 months**. - ☐ Response time to Examination Report: **30 days** (extendable by 1 month). - ☐ Nice Classification: **45 classes** (1–34 goods, 35–45 services). - ☐ Criminal imprisonment for infringement: **Up to 3 years** (6 years for repeat offences). ### **Key Case Laws to Remember** - ☐ *Cadila Pharmaceuticals v. Cadila Healthcare (2001)* — 6-factor test for likelihood of confusion. - ☐ *Reckitt & Colman v. Borden (1990)* — Three-limb test for passing off. - ☐ *Satyam Infoway v. Siffynet Solutions (2004)* — Domain names and trademarks. - ☐ *Milmet Oftho Industries v. Allergan (2004)* — Well-known marks in India. ### **Practical Tips for Students** - ☐ Always **cite Section numbers** when answering IP law questions. - ☐ Use **comparison tables** for "distinguish between" questions — this earns presentation marks. - ☐ Mention at least **one case law** in 7-mark sub-questions to demonstrate legal knowledge. - ☐ For trademark-related questions, emphasize the **commercial and entrepreneurial significance** — it connects to the course outcome (CO1, CO2). - ☐ Remember: **"Trademark = Brand Identity"** — it protects the source, not the invention itself (that's a patent) or the creative expression (that's copyright).

topics.title: Copyrights
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT633 - INNOVATION & ENTREPRENEURSHIP
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Copyrights

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Copyright** is a **statutory, intellectual property right** granted by law to the **creator of original literary, dramatic, musical, artistic, cinematographic, and certain intellectual works**, conferring upon the creator the exclusive right to reproduce, distribute, perform, communicate, and adapt the work for a **limited period of time**, as codified in the **Indian Copyright Act, 1957** (amended in 2012).

Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) perspective, copyrights form a **pillar of the Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) ecosystem** that every engineering innovator and entrepreneur must comprehend before commercialising any creative, software-based, or design-led product.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you build a beautiful **three-storey house** with your own hands, brick by brick. Nobody can walk in, occupy, or replicate your house without your consent — because you hold the **deed (title)**. *Copyright is exactly that "deed" for intangible creative output.* The house = your song/code/novel/diagram. The deed = the copyright. The government registry = the Copyright Office.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Copyright protects the *expression of an idea*, NOT the idea itself. This single distinction decides millions of rupees in tech-litigation outcomes globally.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus-Critical)

- **Author / First Owner** — the natural person who first creates the work (default owner under Section 17 of the Act).
- **Work of Joint Authorship** — a work produced by two or more authors where their contributions are inseparable.
- **Work Made for Hire** — copyright vests in the *employer*, not the employee, for works created in the course of employment under a contract (Section 17, sub-rule).
- **Infringement** — any act done without the licence/permission of the copyright owner that violates the exclusive rights granted under **Section 14**.
- **Fair Dealing** — a statutory defence under **Sections 52 and 52A** allowing limited use for research, criticism, review, news reporting, and education.
- **Berne Convention (1886)** — the international treaty guaranteeing reciprocal copyright protection across **180+ member countries**.

### 1.4 Quick Visual — Where Copyright Sits in the IPR Family

- **Patents** → Inventions (Functional)
- **Trademarks** → Brand Identity (Commercial Source)
- **Industrial Designs** → Aesthetic Appearance of an Article
- **Copyrights** → Literary, Artistic, Musical, Software (Expression)
- **Trade Secrets** → Confidential Know-How
- **Geographical Indications** → Region-based Product Identity

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** "Distinguish between Patent and Copyright" appears in nearly every Innovation & Entrepreneurship module test.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of Copyright — The Five Pillars

**Pillar 1 — Subject Matter (What is Protected)**
Under **Section 13** of the Indian Copyright Act, copyright subsists in the following classes of works:

- **Literary Works** — books, computer programs, compilations, tables (Section 2(o)).
- **Dramatic Works** — scripts, choreography (if recorded) (Section 2(h)).
- **Musical Works** — compositions excluding lyrics (Section 2(p)).
- **Artistic Works** — paintings, drawings, photographs, sculptures, architectural plans (Section 2(c)).
- **Cinematograph Films** — visual recordings + accompanying sound (Section 2(f)).
- **Sound Recordings** — independent of underlying composition (Section 2(xx)).
- **Broadcasts** — radio/TV transmissions (Section 2(t)).

**Pillar 2 — Conditions for Protection**

A work must satisfy **three conditions simultaneously** to attract copyright:

1. **Originality** — must originate from the author's own skill, judgement, and labour (not copied).
2. **Material Form Fixation** — must be recorded in some tangible medium (paper, disk, canvas, RAM).
3. **Minimum Creativity Threshold** — must cross the *de minimis* floor (trivial effort does not qualify).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Idea–Expression Dichotomy:** The Supreme Court of India, in *Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak (2008)*, conclusively settled that copyright does NOT protect ideas, methods, procedures, or facts — only their specific expression.

**Pillar 3 — Bundle of Rights (Economic + Moral)**

Under **Section 14**, the copyright owner enjoys the exclusive right to:

- Reproduce the work in any material form.
- Issue copies to the public (distribution).
- Perform the work in public.
- Communicate the work to the public (broadcast/stream).
- Translate, adapt, or arrange the work.
- Make cinematograph film or sound recording.

**Moral Rights** (Sections 57–58): independent of copyright assignment, the author retains the right to claim authorship and to prevent distortion/mutilation prejudicial to honour/reputation.

**Pillar 4 — Ownership Rules (Section 17)**

| Mode of Creation | Default Owner |
|---|---|
| Independent author | The author |
| Joint authorship | All joint authors (undivided shares) |
| Employee (work in course of employment) | **Employer** |
| Contract for valuable consideration | **The person commissioning** (for specified categories like artistic/photograph/portrait only) |
| Government / International Organisation work | Government / IO |

**Pillar 5 — Duration of Protection (Section 22–29)**

| Work Type | Term of Copyright |
|---|---|
| Literary, Dramatic, Musical, Artistic | **Life of author + 60 years** |
| Cinematograph Films | **60 years from date of publication** |
| Sound Recordings | **60 years from date of publication** |
| Photographs | **60 years from date of publication** |
| Broadcasts | **25 years from date of broadcast** |
| Government / IO works | **60 years from date of publication** |
| Anonymous / Pseudonymous works | **60 years from date of publication** |

> [!NOTE]
> After expiry, the work enters the **Public Domain** — free for all to use without permission or royalty.

### 2.2 KTU Cheat Sheet — Copyrights at a Glance

| Parameter | Key Fact |
|---|---|
| Enacting Statute | Indian Copyright Act, **1957** |
| Latest Amendment | Copyright (Amendment) Act, **2012** |
| International Treaty | **Berne Convention**, 1886 (India acceded 1928) |
| Copyright Office | Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT) |
| Authority | Copyright Board (now merged into IPAB tribunal framework) |
| Registration | Voluntary (not mandatory, but confers prima facie evidence) |
| Symbol Used | © (copyright notice) — not legally required in India but useful |
| Term (Standard) | Life + 60 years |
| Infringement Forum | District Court / Commercial Court |
| Civil Remedy | Damages, injunction, account of profits, delivery-up |
| Criminal Penalty | Up to **3 years imprisonment** + fine (minimum ₹50,000 for first offence) |
| Software Protection | Section 63-B, separate criminal provision for software piracy |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

For an engineering student, copyright governs the protection of:
- Source code and firmware (treated as *literary work*).
- CAD drawings, technical schematics, PCB layouts.
- Technical manuals, datasheets, training videos.
- Architectural blueprints (under artistic works).
- Recorded lectures and MOOC content.

> [!TIP]
> **Open-Source Reality:** Linux Kernel, Android AOSP, ReactJS — these are *copyrighted works distributed under licences* (GPL, Apache, MIT). Using them without complying with licence terms is still copyright infringement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Is Copyright Infringed?

A structured four-step test used by the Indian Courts (post *RG Anand v. Deluxe Films*, 1978, and *Eastern Book Company*, 2008):

**Step 1 — Access Test**
$$P_{access} = \begin{cases} \text{High} & \text{if defendant's work has striking similarity + plaintiff proves defendant had reasonable opportunity to view original} \\ \text{Low} & \text{if no demonstrable contact exists} \end{cases}$$

The plaintiff must first establish that the alleged infringer had *reasonable access* to the original work.

**Step 2 — Substantial Similarity Test**
$$S_{substantial} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot R_i} \geq 0.40 \text{ (judicial threshold, approx.)}$$

Where $W_i$ = weight of each protectable element, $M_i$ = measure of matching, $R_i$ = reference baseline. Courts look at quality, not quantity, of copying.

**Step 3 — Idea–Expression Dichotomy Filter**
If the allegedly copied portion constitutes an *idea, principle, procedure, or unprotectable fact*, the claim collapses regardless of similarity. Only protectable expression survives.

**Step 4 — Defence Ledger Check**
Apply each statutory defence from **Section 52**:
- Fair dealing (research, criticism, review, news, education).
- Incidental inclusion in another work.
- Temporary reproduction in transient/incidental digital storage.
- Backup copies of computer programmes (single archival copy allowed).

**Infringement Verdict Formula:**
$$V_{infringement} = \begin{cases} \text{Yes} & \text{if } (P_{access} = High) \land (S_{substantial} \geq \theta) \land (\text{expression protected}) \land (\text{no Sec. 52 defence}) \\ \text{No} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step — Copyright Registration Process (India)

The Copyright Office (Delhi) follows a structured workflow for voluntary registration:

**Step 1 — Application Filing**
Submit **Form XIV** (for literary/dramatic/musical/artistic works) or **Form XV** (for cinematograph films/sound recordings) under Rule 70 of the Copyright Rules, 2013.

**Step 2 — Statutory Fee Payment**
$$F_{total} = F_{base} + F_{per\_extra\_author}$$

(For 2024: Base fee ₹500 per work; ₹50 per additional author; ₹2,000 per work if filed via e-filing portal *copyright.gov.in* — *no* coding required, all online via the government portal.)

**Step 3 — Diary Number Generation**
The Registrar generates a *Diary Number* (e.g., 12345/2024) within 30 days — this serves as prima facie evidence in court.

**Step 4 — Examination by Examiner**
The application is examined for:
- Correctness of particulars.
- No conflict with prior registered works.
- Compliance with formal requirements under Section 45(1).

**Step 5 — Publication in the Copyright Journal**
If accepted, the entry is published in the official Copyright Journal — the world receives *constructive notice* of your claim.

**Step 6 — Issuance of Registration Certificate**
The Registrar issues a Registration Certificate under Section 45(2), which acts as conclusive evidence of the copyright particulars in all legal proceedings.

**Step 7 — Renewal / Maintenance**
No periodic renewal required — registration lasts the entire copyright term (Life + 60 years for standard works).

### 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step — Identifying the "Work" You Can Copyright

A student's innovation might cross multiple IP boundaries. Use this decision matrix:

$$IP_{route} = \begin{cases} \text{Copyright} & \text{if output is a creative expression fixed in a medium} \\ \text{Patent} & \text{if output is a novel, useful, non-obvious technical invention} \\ \text{Trademark} & \text{if output is a brand name/logo/slogan used in commerce} \\ \text{Design} & \text{if output is an industrial article's visual appearance} \\ \text{Trade Secret} & \text{if output is confidential know-how held internally} \\ \text{Combined} & \text{if output spans multiple categories (e.g., a software UI is copyrighted, patented if novel, trademarked as logo)} \end{cases}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** A piece of firmware may be **(a)** copyrighted as literary code, **(b)** patented as a novel algorithm, AND **(c)** have its UI screens protected as artistic works — simultaneously, by three different IP regimes.

### 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough — Fair Dealing Analysis

When a university professor uses 200 words from a textbook in a slide deck, the test applied is:

- **Purpose** — non-commercial / educational (favours defendant).
- **Nature of Work** — factual vs. creative (factual favours defendant).
- **Quantum Used** — proportionate to purpose.
- **Effect on Market** — does it substitute the original?

If all four tilt favourably, **Section 52(1)(a)** is invoked and no infringement occurs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Copyright Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Idea Conception] --> B{Creative Expression\nin Material Form?}
    B -- No --> C[No Copyright\nPublic Domain]
    B -- Yes --> D[Original Work Created\nby Author]
    D --> E[Copyright Arises\nAutomatically]
    E --> F{Need Stronger\nLegal Proof?}
    F -- No --> G[Use Without\nRegistration]
    F -- Yes --> H[File Form XIV / XV\nwith Copyright Office]
    H --> I[Diary Number Issued\nPrima Facie Evidence]
    I --> J[Examination &\nJournal Publication]
    J --> K[Registration Certificate\nSec 45(2)]
    D --> L[Exclusive Rights\nSec 14 Reproduce]
    D --> M[Exclusive Rights\nSec 14 Distribute]
    D --> N[Exclusive Rights\nSec 14 Communicate]
    D --> O[Exclusive Rights\nSec 14 Adapt]
    L --> P[Term Life Plus 60 Years]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Entry into\nPublic Domain]
```

### 4.2 Copyright Infringement Analytical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alleged Infringement] --> B{Access to Original\nProved?}
    B -- No --> C[No Infringement]
    B -- Yes --> D{Substantial Similarity\nin Protectable Expression?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E{Idea-Expression\nDichotomy Applicable?}
    E -- Yes Idea Only --> C
    E -- No Expression --> F{Section 52\nDefence Available?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Defence Succeeds\nNo Infringement]
    F -- No --> H[Infringement\nEstablished]
    H --> I{Civil or\nCriminal Route?}
    I -- Civil --> J[Injunction +\nDamages + Account]
    I -- Criminal --> K[Imprisonment up to\n3 Years + Fine]
    I -- Both --> L[Parallel Proceedings\nAllowed]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of IP Rights (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IP[Intellectual Property Rights] --> P[Patents]\n(IP1)
    IP --> T[Trademarks]\n(IP2)
    IP --> C[Copyrights]\n(IP3)
    IP --> D[Industrial Designs]\n(IP4)
    IP --> G[Geographical Indications]\n(IP5)
    IP --> TS[Trade Secrets]\n(IP6)
    C --> C1[Literary]
    C --> C2[Artistic]
    C --> C3[Musical]
    C --> C4[Dramatic]
    C --> C5[Cinematograph]
    C --> C6[Sound Recording]
    C --> C7[Software Code]
```

### 4.4 Copyright Ownership Decision Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Who Created the Work?] --> Q1{Type of\nEngagement?}
    Q1 -- Independent --> OA[Author is Owner]
    Q1 -- Employee --> OE[Employer is Owner\nSection 17]
    Q1 -- Commissioned Portrait\nor Photograph --> OC[Commissioner is Owner\nSection 17]
    Q1 -- Joint --> OJ[All Joint Authors\nUndivisible Shares]
    Q1 -- Anonymous --> OG[Publisher for Term\nSection 27]
    Start --> Q2{Nature of Work?}
    Q2 -- Govt of India --> GO[Government Ownership]
    Q2 -- International Org --> IO[International Organisation]
    Q2 -- Public Sector Undertaking --> PSU[PSU Ownership per Charter]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Copyright. List any four classes of work protected under the Indian Copyright Act, 1957.*

**Model Answer:**
Copyright is a statutory intellectual property right granted to the creator of an original work fixed in a tangible medium, conferring exclusive economic and moral rights over the work for a limited period. It is governed by the Indian Copyright Act, 1957 (as amended in 2012).

Four classes of protected work (Section 13):
1. Literary works (books, software, compilations).
2. Musical works (compositions).
3. Artistic works (paintings, drawings, photographs).
4. Cinematograph films and sound recordings.

*(Any four — full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Mention the duration of copyright for (i) literary work, (ii) cinematograph film, and (iii) broadcast under the Indian Copyright Act.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Literary work** — Lifetime of the author + **60 years** post-death (Section 22).
2. **Cinematograph film** — **60 years** from the date of first publication (Section 25).
3. **Broadcast** — **25 years** from the date of broadcast (Section 28).

*(Each item carries 1 mark; mention of Section reference earns the additional keyword mark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **rights conferred on a copyright owner** under Section 14 of the Indian Copyright Act, 1957. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **doctrine of Fair Dealing** as a defence to copyright infringement, with reference to Sections 52 and 52A. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A (a)

The exclusive rights of a copyright owner under **Section 14(1)** are:

1. **Right to Reproduce the Work** — to make one or more copies/photographic reproductions in any material form (paper, digital, microfilm).
2. **Right to Issue Copies to the Public** — to distribute the work, including renting, lending, or selling copies.
3. **Right to Perform the Work in Public** — applies to dramatic, musical, or literary works (live rendition).
4. **Right to Communicate to the Public** — broadcasting, webcasting, streaming, satellite transmission, or any wireless/digital dissemination.
5. **Right to Make Translation / Adaptation** — converting a literary/dramatic work into another language or format.
6. **Right to Make Cinematograph Film / Sound Recording** — adapting literary or musical works into audio-visual or audio-only fixed forms.

These rights are **economic rights**, transferrable by assignment or licence. Additionally, the author retains **moral rights** under **Sections 57–58**:
- Right to claim authorship of the work.
- Right to integrity — to prevent distortion, modification, or mutilation prejudicial to honour or reputation.

Moral rights are **inalienable** and continue to subsist even after assignment of economic rights.

#### Model Solution to Q.A (b)

**Fair Dealing** is a **statutory exception** to copyright infringement, codified in **Section 52(1)(a)** of the Indian Copyright Act. It permits limited use of copyrighted material without the owner's consent, provided certain conditions are met.

**Permitted Purposes under Section 52(1)(a):**
- Private or personal use, including research.
- Criticism or review of the work (with due acknowledgement).
- Reporting of current events or current affairs (with due acknowledgement).
- Non-commercial educational use in a non-profit institution.

**Additional Exception under Section 52(1)(ab):**
- Use within a court or judicial proceeding.

**Section 52A — Fair Dealing for Library and Educational Use:**
- Library/archive may reproduce one copy of a published work for preservation.
- Educational institutions may reproduce for classroom instruction within reasonable limits.

**Judicial Tests Applied:**
1. **Purpose** — is it commercial or non-profit?
2. **Nature of Work** — fact-based (looser protection) or creative (stricter protection)?
3. **Amount Used** — proportionate to the purpose?
4. **Market Effect** — does the use substitute the original?

If all factors favour the user, no infringement arises. The user must, however, **acknowledge the source and author** unless attribution is impossible.

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternate]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Copyright and Patent**. Mention any six distinguishing features. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **moral rights of an author** and the **remedies available for copyright infringement** in India. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B (a)

| Parameter | Copyright | Patent |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Protects expression of an idea | Protects a functional invention |
| **Subject Matter** | Literary, artistic, musical, software | Process, product, method, composition |
| **Registration** | Automatic on creation; voluntary registration | Mandatory grant by Patent Office |
| **Term** | Life + 60 years (literary works) | **20 years** from date of filing |
| **Criteria** | Originality + fixation | Novelty + Inventive step + Industrial application |
| **Authority** | Copyright Office | Controller General of Patents |
| **Statute** | Copyright Act, 1957 | Patents Act, 1970 |
| **Renewal** | Not required (single term) | Annual renewal fees from year 3 |
| **Infringement** | Damages + Injunction + 3 yr imprisonment | Damages + Injunction (no criminal) |

*(Any six well-explained points fetch full 7 marks.)*

#### Model Solution to Q.B (b)

**Moral Rights of an Author (Sections 57 & 58):**
- Independent of economic rights; cannot be assigned but can be waived.
- Right to **claim authorship** of the work (paternity right).
- Right to **integrity** — to restrain any distortion, mutilation, or modification prejudicial to honour or reputation.
- Right to **restrain destruction** of the work if it harms author's honour/reputation (architectural works protected against demolition).
- Subsists even after copyright assignment — enforceable during the entire term of copyright.

**Remedies for Copyright Infringement:**

**Civil Remedies (under the Copyright Act + CPC):**
- **Injunction** (interim or permanent) — to restrain further infringement.
- **Damages** — compensatory, additional, or statutory damages.
- **Account of Profits** — infringer must disgorge profits earned.
- **Delivery-Up** — infringing copies and plates handed over to the owner.
- **Anton Piller Order** — surprise court-ordered search of premises.
- **Mareva Injunction** — freezing of infringer's assets.

**Criminal Remedies (Section 63–70):**
- Imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine (minimum ₹50,000 first offence, ₹1,00,000 subsequent).
- For software piracy specifically under Section 63-B — minimum **7 days imprisonment extendable to 3 years** + fine of ₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000.
- Police may seize infringing copies without warrant under Section 64.
- Cognizable and non-bailable offence for repeat offences.

**Customs Remedies:**
- Customs authorities can seize imported infringing goods on owner's notice.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students confuse **Patent with Copyright** — remember: Patent = invention (how it works), Copyright = expression (the way it is presented).
> 2. Writing "copyright is for life + 50 years" — wrong; it is **Life + 60 years** under the 2012 amendment.
> 3. Forgetting that **moral rights are non-transferable but waivable** — only economic rights are assignable.
> 4. Omitting **Section 52-A** library exception when discussing fair dealing — costs 2 marks.
> 5. Not citing the **statute name (Indian Copyright Act, 1957)** properly — examiners explicitly allocate marks for correct legal citation.
> 6. Treating copyright as automatic + worldwide — it is automatic in India but depends on **Berne Convention reciprocity** for foreign protection.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Copyright** is a *statutory intellectual property right* over original expression — protects form, not idea.
- **Automatic on creation** — no registration required for subsistence, but voluntary registration confers prima facie evidence.
- **Statute:** Indian Copyright Act, **1957** (amended 2012).
- **Duration:** Literary/Dramatic/Musical/Artistic = **Life + 60 years**; Cinematograph/Sound Recording = **60 years from publication**; Broadcast = **25 years**.
- **Exclusive Rights under Section 14:** Reproduce, Distribute, Perform, Communicate, Translate, Adapt, Make film/recording.
- **Ownership under Section 17:** Default = author; Employee = employer; Specific commissioned works = commissioner.
- **Idea–Expression Dichotomy** is the bedrock defence — *Eastern Book Co. v. D.B. Modak (2008)*.
- **Fair Dealing (Section 52)** — research, criticism, review, news, education; *with attribution*.
- **Moral Rights (Sections 57–58):** non-transferable, non-assignable, but waivable.
- **Software is a literary work** under Indian law — Section 63-B criminalises software piracy with mandatory minimum penalties.
- **Injunction + Damages + Account + Delivery-up** = civil remedies; **Imprisonment + Fine** = criminal remedies.
- **Berne Convention (1886)** — India is a signatory since 1928; secures automatic international protection for Indian works.
- **Post-term expiry** = entry into **public domain** — free for all to use.
- **Engineering applications:** firmware, PCB layouts, technical manuals, MOOC lectures, CAD drawings, AI model weights (under evolving jurisprudence).
- **Distinct from Patent:** Patent = functional invention (20 yrs); Copyright = creative expression (Life + 60 yrs).
- **Distinct from Trademark:** Trademark = commercial source identifier (10 yrs renewable indefinitely).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grassroots Innovation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT633 - INNOVATION & ENTREPRENEURSHIP > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Grassroots Innovation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Grassroots Innovation (GRI)** is defined as a network of **bottom-up**, decentralized, community-driven innovation processes in which marginalized, resource-constrained, or rural stakeholders create novel solutions using **locally available resources**, indigenous knowledge systems, and frugal engineering principles to address pressing socio-economic challenges within their immediate ecosystems. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Grassroots Innovation is positioned at the intersection of **inclusive innovation**, **frugal engineering**, and **social entrepreneurship**. It is sometimes referred to as **"Jugaad Innovation"** in the Indian context — a Hindi/Punjabi term denoting an improvised, flexible, low-cost workaround or hack to solve a real-world problem. **Related Formal Synonyms used in KTU Syllabus:** - **Bottom-of-the-Pyramid (BoP) Innovation** - **Frugal Innovation** - **Inclusive Innovation** - **Reverse Innovation** (when solutions migrate from developing to developed markets) - **Jugaad Innovation** (India-specific term coined by Navi Radjou, Jaideep Prabhu & Simone Ahuja) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Village Carpenter Analogy** 🔧 > Imagine a village carpenter in rural Kerala who cannot afford an imported ₹50,000 automatic wood-cutting machine, but invents a bicycle-pedal-powered wooden lathe from scrap parts to cut timber precisely for his community. He did not attend an IIT, has no R&D budget, and no patent attorney — yet he has **solved a real problem with locally available resources**. > > This is the essence of **Grassroots Innovation**: ordinary people, using extraordinary creativity, solving problems that global corporations often overlook because the "market is too small" or the "consumers are too poor." ### 1.3 Core Distinguishing Metrics (Bold Constants) | Metric | Value / Description | |---|---| | **Cost Reduction Target** | Typically **>90%** cheaper than conventional alternatives | | **Target User Base** | Bottom **3–4 billion** people at the Base of the Pyramid (BoP) | | **Innovation Source** | **End-users / community members**, not R&D labs | | **Resource Base** | **Local, recycled, or indigenous materials** | | **Key Indian Institution** | **Honey Bee Network, NInC, GIAN, SRISTI** | ### 1.4 Why Grassroots Innovation Matters to KTU Engineers As future B.Tech graduates in Kerala, students will often deploy technology in resource-constrained environments (villages, MSMEs, flood-relief zones, start-up incubators). Understanding GRI equips engineers to design **affordable, accessible, and sustainable** solutions aligned with the **UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** and India's **Atmanirbhar Bharat** mission. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Conceptual Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations Grassroots Innovation rests on **five intersecting theoretical pillars**. Each pillar must be understood for KTU exam answers: #### Pillar 1 — Frugal Engineering Principle - "Doing more with less" for the many, not the "less for the few." - It is **not** about compromising quality; it is about **re-engineering functionality** to deliver core value at a radically lower cost. - **Example:** **Mitticool** — a clay refrigerator developed by Mansukhbhai Prajapati (Gujarat) that costs ₹2,500 vs. a conventional fridge at ₹15,000, using only clay, water, and electricity for cooling. #### Pillar 2 — Bottom of the Pyramid (BoP) Framework - Coined by **C.K. Prahalad** in his seminal 2004 book *The Fortune at the Bottom of the Pyramid*. - The BoP represents the **largest, poorest, and most underserved** socio-economic segment. - BoP consumers are not "passive victims" — they are **active value creators** and **innovators themselves**. #### Pillar 3 — Jugaad Mindset - **Jugaad** = flexible, low-cost, improvised solution born out of constraint. - Six characteristics (Radjou, Prabhu, Ahuja, 2012): 1. **Seek opportunity in adversity** 2. **Do more with less** 3. **Think and act flexibly** 4. **Keep it simple** 5. **Include the margin** 6. **Follow your heart** #### Pillar 4 — Indigenous Knowledge Systems (IKS) - Traditional, community-held knowledge passed down orally across generations. - Examples: Ayurvedic medicine, traditional water harvesting (e.g., Kerala's *Kulavadi*), organic pest control in Wayanad's cardamom plantations. #### Pillar 5 — Reverse Innovation Flow - Innovation originating in **developing economies (like India)** that later scales to **developed markets** (USA, EU, Japan). - Example: GE's **MACi** portable ECG machine (designed in India for rural use, later adopted globally). ### 2.2 The Grassroots Innovation Value Chain (5-Stage Model) | Stage | Activity | Key Actor | |---|---|---| | **1. Problem Identification** | Local unmet need is recognized | Community member / villager | | **2. Idea Generation** | Improvised, low-cost solution conceived | Individual innovator / artisan | | **3. Prototyping & Testing** | Solution built using local materials, refined iteratively | Innovator + local mentors | | **4. Validation & Diffusion** | Honey Bee Network / SRISTI / GIAN documents and validates | Support institutions | | **5. Scaling & Commercialization** | Tie-ups with MSMEs, start-ups, or social enterprises | Industry / Government / NGOs | ### 2.3 Key Indian Institutional Ecosystem (KTU High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > **Memorize this 4-organization cluster — it appears frequently in KTU 14-mark questions:** - **Honey Bee Network** (founded 1988 by Prof. Anil K.
