# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Operations Management. List its three primary objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Operations Management (OM) is the administration of business practices to create the highest level of efficiency possible within an organization. It involves the design, operation, and improvement of the production systems that create the organization's products or services.

**Three Primary Objectives:**
1. **Customer Satisfaction:** Delivering quality products/services on time
2. **Cost Efficiency:** Minimizing operational costs while maximizing productivity
3. **Competitive Advantage:** Creating unique value through operational excellence

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1.5 marks) + Objectives (1.5 marks)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between Job Shop Production and Continuous Production.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Job Shop Production | Continuous Production |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Volume** | Low | Very High |
| **Variety** | High | Very Low |
| **Flow** | Intermittent | Continuous |
| **Equipment** | General purpose | Specialized |
| **Unit Cost** | High | Very Low |
| **Examples** | Custom furniture, ship repair | Oil refinery, cement plant |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks - Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain the concept of **Total Quality Management (TQM)** in detail. Discuss its key principles and implementation framework. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive management philosophy that focuses on continuously improving all organizational processes to deliver superior value to customers.

**Key Principles of TQM:**

1. **Customer Focus:** Quality is defined by customer requirements. Organizations must understand and meet customer needs to ensure satisfaction.

2. **Total Employee Involvement:** All employees, from top management to frontline workers, must participate in quality improvement initiatives.

3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** Never-ending effort to improve products, services, and processes. Small, incremental improvements compound over time.

4. **Process-Centered Approach:** Focus on improving processes rather than blaming individuals. A well-defined process produces consistent quality.

5. **Integrated System:** All departments (production, marketing, finance, HR) must work cohesively as a unified system.

6. **Strategic and Systematic Approach:** Quality management must be integrated into the strategic plan, not treated as an isolated function.

7. **Fact-Based Decision Making:** Use data, statistical analysis, and performance metrics rather than intuition.

8. **Communication:** Effective communication at all levels to maintain morale and motivation during change.

9. **Supplier Partnership:** Build long-term relationships with suppliers based on mutual trust and quality standards.

10. **Training and Education:** Continuous skill development ensures employees can contribute effectively.

**Implementation Framework:**

**Step 1:** Top management commitment and leadership
**Step 2:** Establish quality vision and policies
**Step 3:** Employee training and awareness programs
**Step 4:** Form quality circles and improvement teams
**Step 5:** Implement quality measurement systems (SPC)
**Step 6:** Benchmark against industry best practices
**Step 7:** Continuous monitoring and refinement

**Tools Used in TQM:**
- Pareto Charts
- Cause-and-Effect Diagrams (Ishikawa/Fishbone)
- Control Charts
- Flowcharts
- Check Sheets

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + 10 Principles (3) + Implementation (2) + Tools (1)

---

**(b)** A company uses **12,000 units** of raw material annually. The ordering cost is **₹600 per order** and the holding cost is **₹15 per unit per year**. Calculate:
   (i) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)
   (ii) Number of orders per year
   (iii) Total annual inventory cost

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $D = 12{,}000$ units/year
- $S = ₹600$ per order
- $H = ₹15$ per unit/year

**(i) EOQ Calculation:**

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 600}{15}}$$

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{14{,}400{,}000}{15}}$$

$$EOQ = \sqrt{960{,}000}$$

$$EOQ \approx 980 \text{ units}$$

**(ii) Number of Orders per Year:**

$$N = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{12{,}000}{980} \approx 12.24 \approx 12 \text{ orders}$$

**(iii) Total Annual Inventory Cost:**

$$\text{Ordering Cost} = \frac{D}{Q} \times S = \frac{12{,}000}{980} \times 600 = ₹7{,}347$$

$$\text{Holding Cost} = \frac{Q}{2} \times H = \frac{980}{2} \times 15 = ₹7{,}350$$

$$TC = ₹7{,}347 + ₹7{,}350 = ₹14{,}697$$

**Alternative Calculation using $TC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$:**

$$TC_{min} = \sqrt{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 600 \times 15} = \sqrt{216{,}000{,}000} = ₹14{,}697$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** EOQ formula (2) + Calculation (2) + Number of orders (1) + Total cost (2)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] - Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **Lean Manufacturing** philosophy. Explain its seven types of waste (Muda) with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Lean Manufacturing** is a systematic methodology for waste minimization without sacrificing productivity. Originating from the Toyota Production System (TPS), it focuses on creating value for customers while eliminating non-value-adding activities.

**Core Philosophy:** "Do more with less" — less human effort, less equipment, less time, less space — while delivering exactly what the customer needs.

**Seven Types of Waste (TIMWOOD + 1):**

| Waste (Japanese) | Description | Example |
|------------------|-------------|---------|
| **Transport** (Mura) | Unnecessary movement of materials | Moving parts between distant workstations |
| **Inventory** (Muda) | Excess raw materials/WIP/finished goods | Overstocking perishable goods |
| **Motion** (Unecessary) | Unnecessary human movement | Searching for tools in disorganized workplace |
| **Waiting** (Idle time) | Idle time due to delays | Workers waiting for machine repair |
| **Overproduction** | Producing more than demand | Manufacturing items without confirmed orders |
| **Over-processing** | Doing more work than necessary | Excess polishing when customer doesn't require |
| **Defects** | Products requiring rework/scrap | Manufacturing errors causing warranty claims |
| **Skills (unused talent)** | Wasting human potential | Assigning engineers to repetitive tasks |

**Lean Tools and Techniques:**

1. **5S Methodology:** Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain
2. **Value Stream Mapping (VSM):** Visualizing entire material and information flow
3. **Kanban System:** Visual signals for production control
4. **Poka-Yoke:** Mistake-proofing devices
5. **Just-in-Time (JIT):** Producing only what is needed, when needed
6. **Kaizen:** Continuous incremental improvements
7. **Jidoka:** Autonomation — machines detect defects automatically

**Benefits of Lean Manufacturing:**
- Reduced lead times (30-90% reduction)
- Lower inventory costs (50% reduction possible)
- Improved quality (fewer defects)
- Enhanced flexibility and responsiveness
- Higher employee morale

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Lean definition (1) + 7 wastes with examples (4) + Tools (2)

---

**(b)** A startup company is launching a new product with the following cost structure:
- **Fixed Costs:** ₹8,00,000
- **Variable Cost per Unit:** ₹1,500
- **Selling Price per Unit:** ₹2,500

Calculate:
   (i) Break-Even Point in units
   (ii) Break-Even Point in revenue
   (iii) Profit if 1,000 units are sold

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $FC = ₹8{,}00{,}000$
- $v = ₹1{,}500$ per unit
- $P = ₹2{,}500$ per unit

**(i) Break-Even Point (Units):**

$$BEP_{units} = \frac{FC}{P - v}$$

$$BEP_{units} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{2{,}500 - 1{,}500}$$

$$BEP_{units} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{1{,}000}$$

$$BEP_{units} = 800 \text{ units}$$

**(ii) Break-Even Point (Revenue):**

$$BEP_{revenue} = \frac{FC}{1 - \frac{v}{P}}$$

$$BEP_{revenue} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{1 - \frac{1{,}500}{2{,}500}}$$

$$BEP_{revenue} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{1 - 0.6}$$

$$BEP_{revenue} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{0.4}$$

$$BEP_{revenue} = ₹20{,}00{,}000$$

**(iii) Profit at 1,000 Units:**

$$\text{Total Revenue} = 1{,}000 \times 2{,}500 = ₹25{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Total Cost} = 8{,}00{,}000 + (1{,}000 \times 1{,}500) = 8{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 = ₹23{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Profit} = ₹25{,}00{,}000 - ₹23{,}00{,}000 = ₹2{,}00{,}000$$

**Verification using contribution margin:**

$$\text{Contribution per unit} = 2{,}500 - 1{,}500 = ₹1{,}000$$

$$\text{Units above BEP} = 1{,}000 - 800 = 200 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{Profit} = 200 \times 1{,}000 = ₹2{,}00{,}000 ✓$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** BEP formula (1.5) + Calculation units (1.5) + Revenue (1.5) + Profit with verification (2.5)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **EOQ Problems:** Students often forget to take square root. Always show the full calculation chain.
> 2. **Break-Even:** Confusing "units above BEP" with "total units sold." Profit = (Units Sold − BEP) × Contribution Margin.
> 3. **TQM Questions:** Writing principles without examples loses marks. KTU examiners award marks for *application*, not just *recall*.
> 4. **Lean Manufacturing:** Listing wastes without Japanese terminology (Muda, Mura, etc.) is incomplete.
> 5. **Missing Units:** Always write units in final answers (units, ₹, %, etc.).
> 6. **No Diagrams:** For 7-mark questions, always include a diagram or flowchart for full marks.
> 7. **Formula without Derivation:** Writing $EOQ = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ without explaining $D$, $S$, $H$ leads to partial marking.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — Operations Management**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ **Definition:** OM = Efficient conversion of inputs to outputs
- ✅ **5 Ms of Inputs:** Men, Machine, Material, Method, Money
- ✅ **3 Objectives:** Customer satisfaction, cost efficiency, competitive advantage
- ✅ **10 Strategic Decisions:** Design, Quality, Process, Location, Layout, HR, SCM, Inventory, Scheduling, Maintenance

### Critical Formulas
- ✅ **Productivity:** $\text{Output} / \text{Input}$
- ✅ **EOQ:** $\sqrt{2DS/H}$
- ✅ **Total Inventory Cost:** $\frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2}$
- ✅ **Break-Even Units:** $\frac{FC}{P - v}$
- ✅ **Takt Time:** $\frac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$
- ✅ **Six Sigma:** 3.4 DPMO target
- ✅ **Pareto Principle:** 80/20 rule
- ✅ **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda W$

### Frameworks to Remember
- ✅ **TQM:** Customer focus + Continuous improvement + Employee involvement
- ✅ **Lean (TIMWOOD + 1):** Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects, Skills
- ✅ **Six Sigma:** DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control)
- ✅ **Process Types:** Job Shop → Batch → Assembly Line → Continuous Flow
- ✅ **3 Vs:** Volume, Variety, Variation
- ✅ **5S:** Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain

### Practical Applications
- ✅ EOQ balances ordering cost with holding cost
- ✅ Break-even determines minimum viable sales
- ✅ Lean eliminates 8 types of waste
- ✅ TQM requires top management commitment
- ✅ Capacity planning anticipates demand fluctuations
- ✅ Inventory decisions: EOQ for independent, MRP for dependent demand

### Entrepreneurial Connection
- ✅ OM bridges innovation and commercialization
- ✅ Startups must master MVP-to-scale operations
- ✅ Lean startup methodology = Lean manufacturing applied to business
- ✅ BEP is critical for investor pitches and financial viability
- ✅ Quality management builds brand reputation and customer loyalty

> [!TIP]
> **🎓 Final Exam Strategy:**
> 1. For **3-mark questions:** Definition + 2-3 key points + Example
> 2. For **7-mark sub-questions:** Diagram + Step-by-step explanation + Formula with derivation
> 3. For **14-mark questions:** Two sub-parts with clear headings, diagrams, and numerical examples
> 4. Always **label diagrams** and **state assumptions** explicitly
> 5. Use **markdown-style organization** in written answers (bullet points, tables)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Personnel Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT633 - INNOVATION & ENTREPRENEURSHIP
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Personnel Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Syllabus Definition
> **Personnel Management (PM)** is the administrative discipline of hiring, developing, and supporting employees so that they can perform their jobs effectively within the framework of an organization's strategic objectives. In the context of **Innovation & Entrepreneurship (PEBBT633)**, Personnel Management is the entrepreneurial function of assembling, motivating, and retaining the *right mix of human capital* that converts an innovative idea into a sustainable enterprise.

The term is often used interchangeably with **Human Resource Management (HRM)**, though modern KTU textbooks treat Personnel Management as the *operational* subset (recruitment, payroll, welfare, records) and HRM as the *strategic* superset (culture, talent strategy, change management).

> [!NOTE] Key Terminology Locked In
> - **Human Capital**: The collective economic value of employee skills, knowledge, experience, and创新能力 (innovative capability).
> - **Manpower Planning (MPP)**: Forecasting future human-resource needs and preparing a supply pipeline.
> - **Talent Acquisition**: The end-to-end process of identifying, attracting, and onboarding skilled personnel.
> - **Knowledge Worker**: A person whose primary contribution is intellectual capital (highly relevant to innovation-driven startups).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Concert Orchestra" Model

Imagine you are the **conductor of a 60-piece orchestra**. You do not play every instrument yourself. Your job is to:
1. **Audition** musicians (recruitment & selection).
2. **Train** them on a shared musical vocabulary (induction & training).
3. **Give them sheet music and a beat** (job description & performance goals).
4. **Make sure the first violinist, the drummer, and the cellist play in sync** (team building & motivation).
5. **Resolve disputes** when the trumpeter and the flutist disagree (grievance & conflict handling).
6. **Pay each musician fairly** so that the best ones do not leave for another orchestra (compensation & retention).

Personnel Management is the **conductor's job** in a company. A startup founder may begin by "playing every instrument," but as the venture scales, **systematic personnel management** becomes the engine that keeps every department playing in harmony and in tune with the entrepreneur's vision.

## 1.3 Scope & Nature of Personnel Management

The scope spans *procurement*, *development*, *compensation*, and *integration* of people.

> [!TIP] Why this matters for an Innovator / Entrepreneur
> According to KTU 2024 Module 4 outcomes, the ability to **build, lead, and sustain a team** is graded as a **CO4 (Apply-level)** competency. Your innovation will fail to scale if you cannot convert brilliant individuals into a coordinated entrepreneurial team.

**Nature of Personnel Management:**

- **People-oriented**: It deals with the most complex variable — human beings.
- **Continuous process**: From manpower planning to retirement, it is a never-ending cycle.
- **Pervasive function**: Applies to every department (marketing, R&D, finance, production).
- **Developmental focus**: Aims at both individual growth and organizational growth.
- **Strategic partner**: In modern KTU pedagogy, PM is no longer a "back-office" function — it is a strategic lever for innovation.
- **Future-oriented**: It anticipates future talent needs rather than just reacting to vacancies.
- **Integrative discipline**: Combines psychology, sociology, economics, and law.

## 1.4 Personnel Management vs. Human Resource Management

| Dimension | Personnel Management (PM) | Human Resource Management (HRM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Traditional, administrative | Strategic, transformational |
| **Focus** | Routine transactions (payroll, records) | Talent, culture, change |
| **Employee view** | Cost / input | Asset / investment |
| **Time horizon** | Short-term, operational | Long-term, strategic |
| **Management role** | Reactive | Proactive / future-shaping |
| **Key activities** | Welfare, wages, working conditions | Learning, innovation, employer branding |
| **KTU relevance in PEBBT633** | Workforce assembly for the startup | Building a learning, innovative organization |

> [!NOTE] For KTU 2024 Board Exams: When asked the *difference*, write at least 4 points and clearly mark one as "Strategic vs. Operational" — examiners award full marks for this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operative Logic — Functions of Personnel Management

Personnel Management is traditionally broken into **Managerial Functions** and **Operative Functions**.

### 2.1.1 Managerial Functions (performed by top management)

1. **Planning** — Forecasting manpower needs, designing org structure.
2. **Organizing** — Designing jobs, departments, reporting lines.
3. **Directing** — Leading, motivating, communicating vision.
4. **Controlling** — Performance appraisals, audits, compliance.
5. **Staffing** — Acquiring, developing, retaining the right people.

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Exam Tip
> "Managerial functions of PM" is a **3-mark favorite**. Memorize the acronym **P-O-D-C-S** = Planning, Organizing, Directing, Controlling, Staffing. Examiners award full marks for neat ordering.

### 2.1.2 Operative Functions (performed by the HR/personnel department)

1. **Manpower Planning** — Demand–supply forecasting.
2. **Recruitment** — Attracting a pool of candidates.
3. **Selection** — Choosing the best fit.
4. **Placement & Induction** — Onboarding the new hire.
5. **Training & Development** — Skill upgradation.
6. **Performance Appraisal** — Evaluating and rewarding performance.
7. **Compensation Management** — Wage, salary, incentives, benefits.
8. **Welfare & Social Security** — Health, safety, recreation, insurance.
9. **Industrial Relations** — Trade unions, collective bargaining, dispute resolution.
10. **Records & Research** — HR analytics, personnel records, HRIS.

## 2.2 Manpower Planning — The Core Quantitative Function

**Manpower Planning (MPP)** is the process of estimating the *right number* and *right kind* of personnel required at the *right time* and *right place*. It is the single highest-weightage analytical subtopic in KTU Module 4.

**The MPP equation (textbook standard):**

$$
\text{Net Manpower Requirement} = \text{Future Demand} - \text{Future Supply} + \text{Buffer for Attrition}
$$

Or, more formally:

$$
M_{net} = (D_{f} - S_{f}) + B_{attrition}
$$

Where:
- $M_{net}$ = net additional hires required
- $D_{f}$ = forecasted manpower demand (number of people)
- $S_{f}$ = forecasted internal supply (existing staff expected to be available)
- $B_{attrition}$ = buffer for expected resignations, retirements, transfers

### 2.2.1 Demand Forecasting Techniques

| Technique | Description | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **Trend Analysis / Time-Series** | Uses historical manpower data and projects the future | Mature, stable firms |
| **Ratio / Workload Analysis** | Salesmen $= k \times \text{sales volume}$ or nurses $= k \times \text{beds}$ | Service-sector firms |
| **Delphi Technique** | Iterative anonymous expert consensus | R&D-heavy innovation firms |
| **Management Judgement** | Top-management estimates based on business plans | Startups and SMEs |
| **Regression Analysis** | $D = a + bX$ where $X$ is a business driver (output, revenue) | Data-rich firms |

### 2.2.2 Supply Forecasting Techniques

- **Markov Analysis** (most common in KTU problems — see Section 3)
- **Replacement Charts / Succession Plans**
- **Skills Inventories**
- **Internal Mobility & Promotion Data**

## 2.3 Recruitment — The Procurement Function

**Recruitment** = *positive* process of *attracting* more and better candidates.

**Sources of Recruitment:**

1. **Internal Sources** — Promotions, transfers, re-employment of ex-employees, employee referrals.
   - *Advantages:* Cheap, fast, motivating, known performance.
   - *Disadvantages:* Limits fresh talent, creates "succession vacuum."
2. **External Sources** — Campus placements, advertisements, employment exchanges, walk-ins, recruitment agencies, e-recruitment (LinkedIn, Naukri, Internshala), poaching.
   - *Advantages:* Wide choice, fresh skills, diversity.
   - *Disadvantages:* Costly, time-consuming, uncertain fit.

> [!NOTE] KTU Insight: For an entrepreneurial venture, the *founder's network* and *equity-linked hiring* (offering ESOPs) are powerful external-yet-internal sources — a favorite KTU concept question.

## 2.4 Selection — The Procurement–Finalization Function

**Selection** = *negative* process of *rejection* — only the best are selected.

The classic 7-step selection process (must memorize for KTU):

1. **Application Screening** (Initial screening of resumes)
2. **Preliminary Interview** (Telephonic/HR round)
3. **Selection Tests** (Aptitude, personality, psychomotor)
4. **Employment Interview** (In-depth face-to-face)
5. **Reference & Background Checks**
6. **Medical Examination** (Fitness for the job)
7. **Final Selection & Appointment Letter**

## 2.5 Training & Development — The Capacity-Building Function

Training = *Short-term, job-specific* skill enhancement.
Development = *Long-term, general* capability enhancement.

**Identification of Training Needs (3-tier model):**

$$
T_{need} = (S_{desired} - S_{current}) \times P_{relevance}
$$

Where:
- $T_{need}$ = magnitude of training gap
- $S_{desired}$ = standard / desired skill level
- $S_{current}$ = existing skill level
- $P_{relevance}$ = probability that the gap is the cause of poor performance (0 to 1)

**Methods of Training:**

- **On-the-Job (OJT):** Job rotation, coaching, apprenticeship, mentoring.
- **Off-the-Job:** Lectures, case studies, role-plays, vestibule training, simulations, e-learning.

## 2.6 Performance Appraisal — The Control Function

**Performance Appraisal (PA)** is the systematic evaluation of employee performance to facilitate decisions on *promotion, training, compensation, and feedback*.

**Common Methods (group them for KTU):**

| Category | Methods |
|---|---|
| **Traditional** | Ranking, Grading, Graphic Rating Scale, Critical Incident, Checklist, Forced Distribution, Essay Method, Field Review |
| **Modern** | 360° Feedback, Management by Objectives (MBO), Behaviorally Anchored Rating Scale (BARS), Assessment Centre, 720° Feedback |

**Errors in Performance Appraisal (high-weightage KTU topic):**

- **Halo Effect** — One good trait biases all ratings.
- **Horn Effect** — One bad trait biases all ratings.
- **Central Tendency** — Rating everyone as average.
- **Recency Effect** — Only recent events remembered.
- **Personal Bias / Stereotyping** — Judging by gender, caste, religion, etc.
- **Strictness / Leniency Bias** — Being too harsh or too soft.
- **Similar-to-me Bias** — Favoring those who resemble the appraiser.

## 2.7 Compensation Management

**Components of Compensation:**

1. **Wages / Salary** (Basic pay)
2. **DA / Cost-of-Living Allowance** (Inflation hedge)
3. **Incentives / Bonuses** (Performance-linked)
4. **Fringe Benefits / Perks** (HRA, medical, LTA, car, phone)
5. **Perquisites / Executive Perks** (Club membership, stock options, ESOPs)
6. **Social Security & Welfare** (PF, ESI, Gratuity, Insurance)

**Wage Determinants:**

$$
W = f(C, P, M, U, L, T)
$$

Where: $C$ = cost of living, $P$ = productivity, $M$ = market wage rate, $U$ = union pressure, $L$ = legal minimum wage, $T$ = organizational ability to pay.

> [!NOTE] KTU 2024 Innovation Track: For entrepreneurial ventures, **ESOPs (Employee Stock Ownership Plans)** and **sweat equity** are unconventional yet highly testable compensation instruments.

## 2.8 Industrial Relations & Conflict Management

**Industrial Relations (IR)** = the relationship between *employers, employees, trade unions,* and *government* for the smooth functioning of the enterprise.

**Dispute Settlement Machinery in India (essential for KTU):**

1. **Bipartite negotiation** (workplace level)
2. ** Conciliation Officer** (under the Ministry of Labour)
3. **Court of Inquiry**
4. **Voluntary Arbitration**
5. **Adjudication / Labour Court / Industrial Tribunal / National Tribunal**

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP] Cheat-Sheet Table — Pin this for last-minute revision

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Net Manpower Requirement | $M_{net} = (D_{f} - S_{f}) + B_{attrition}$ | Number of persons |
| Training Need Magnitude | $T_{need} = (S_{desired} - S_{current}) \times P_{relevance}$ | Skill-gap score |
| Workload-based Demand | $D = \dfrac{\text{Workload} \times \text{Cycle Time}}{T_{available}}$ | Persons |
| Attrition Rate (Annual) | $A_{rate} = \dfrac{L \times 100}{N_{avg}}$ | $L$ = left, $N_{avg}$ = avg strength, in % |
| Absenteeism Rate | $Abs_{rate} = \dfrac{\text{Man-days lost} \times 100}{\text{Man-days scheduled}}$ | in % |
| Labour Productivity | $P_{labour} = \dfrac{\text{Total Output (₹)}}{\text{Total Man-hours}}$ | ₹/man-hour |
| Wage-to-Cost Ratio | $W_{ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Total Wage Bill} \times 100}{\text{Total Operating Cost}}$ | in % |
| Time-to-Fill (recruitment efficiency) | $T_{fill} = T_{req} - T_{onboard}$ | Days |
| Yield Ratio | $Y_{r} = \dfrac{N_{hired}}{N_{applied}} \times 100$ | in % |
| ROI on Training (Phillips Model) | $ROI_{T} = \dfrac{(\text{Benefit} - \text{Cost}) \times 100}{\text{Cost}}$ | in % |
| Markov Steady-State Supply | $\pi_{i} = \sum_{j} \pi_{j} p_{ji}$ | Probability vector |
| Cost-Per-Hire | $CPH = \dfrac{\text{Total Recruitment Cost}}{N_{hired}}$ | ₹/hire |

> [!WARNING] Do **not** confuse "attrition rate" with "turnover rate." Attrition = people leaving voluntarily/retiring. Turnover includes all separations (voluntary + involuntary). KTU board markers are strict on this.

## 2.10 Real-World Utility for the Engineer-Entrepreneur

Personnel management is *the* operational backbone of an entrepreneurial venture. Specifically:

- **Startup Founding**: You cannot raise Series A funding without showing investors a credible *core team* — PM provides the discipline.
- **Scaling**: Every 10× growth event doubles headcount, triples coordination needs, and quadruples culture risk — PM systems absorb this shock.
- **Innovation Output**: Studies (cited in the KTU syllabus) show that *training and recognition intensity* explains 30–40 % of variance in patent filings per R&D employee.
- **Crisis Resilience (e.g., COVID)**: Firms with mature PM systems adapted 2–3× faster (remote-work, upskilling, mental-wellness programs).

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE] This section is built for the KTU 14-mark question. The derivations are shown *exhaustively* with **no skipped algebraic steps** — exactly the level expected by a KTU board examiner.

## 3.1 Derivation: Workload-Based Manpower Demand

**The problem we are solving:** *"A textile unit produces 1,20,000 metres of fabric in a 240-day working year. Each operator produces 50 m/day. The operator is available for work on 92% of the days. Find the optimal number of operators required."*

**Step 1 — Define the demand equation in its most general form.**

$$
D = \frac{W \times C_{time}}{T_{available} \times E_{att}}
$$

Where:
- $D$ = number of operators required (persons)
- $W$ = total workload (metres of fabric per year)
- $C_{time}$ = standard cycle time per metre (days per metre)
- $T_{available}$ = total working days in the year
- $E_{att}$ = attendance efficiency (decimal form)

**Step 2 — Substitute the textual values into the general formula.**

Given: $W = 1{,}20{,}000$ m, $T_{available} = 240$ days, output per operator per day = 50 m ⇒ cycle time per metre = $1/50$ day.

$$
D = \frac{1{,}20{,}000 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{50}\right)}{240 \times 0.92}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator first.**

$$
1{,}20{,}000 \times \frac{1}{50} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{50} = 2{,}400 \text{ operator-days}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
240 \times 0.92 = 220.8 \text{ effective operator-days per year}
$$

**Step 5 — Divide to obtain $D$.**

$$
D = \frac{2{,}400}{220.8} = 10.869...
$$

**Step 6 — Round up to the next whole person (you cannot have 0.87 of an operator).**

$$
D \approx 11 \text{ operators}
$$

> [!TIP] The rounding-up rule is called the **"ceiling function"** $D = \lceil x \rceil$. In KTU exams, never write 10.87 — write **11 operators** and add the one-line justification.

## 3.2 Derivation: Markov-Chain Manpower Supply Forecasting

**Problem:** *"A firm has 60 workers at level L1 (Worker) and 40 at level L2 (Supervisor). Yearly transition probabilities are: P(L1→L1) = 0.7, P(L1→L2) = 0.2, P(L1→Quit) = 0.1; P(L2→L2) = 0.8, P(L2→L1) = 0.05, P(L2→Quit) = 0.15. Find the steady-state workforce composition."*

**Step 1 — Build the Transition Probability Matrix (TPM) $P$.**

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.2 & 0.1 \\ 0.05 & 0.8 & 0.15 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Rows: L1, L2, Quit (absorbing). Columns: L1, L2, Quit.

**Step 2 — Set up the steady-state equation.**

The steady-state probability vector $\pi = (\pi_1, \pi_2, \pi_3)$ satisfies:

$$
\pi P = \pi, \quad \sum_{i} \pi_i = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Write out the system of equations.**

From the first row: $\pi_1 = 0.7\pi_1 + 0.05\pi_2 + 0\pi_3$

From the second row: $\pi_2 = 0.2\pi_1 + 0.8\pi_2 + 0\pi_3$

From the third row (Quit is absorbing): $\pi_3 = 0.1\pi_1 + 0.15\pi_2 + 1 \cdot \pi_3$ — this is automatic.

**Step 4 — Simplify the first two equations.**

First: $\pi_1 - 0.7\pi_1 = 0.05\pi_2 \Rightarrow 0.3\pi_1 = 0.05\pi_2 \Rightarrow \pi_2 = 6\pi_1$

Second: $\pi_2 - 0.8\pi_2 = 0.2\pi_1 \Rightarrow 0.2\pi_2 = 0.2\pi_1 \Rightarrow \pi_2 = \pi_1$

**Step 5 — Cross-check consistency.** The two derived relations ($\pi_2 = 6\pi_1$ and $\pi_2 = \pi_1$) seem inconsistent. *Resolution:* For a workforce with an absorbing state, the true steady state in the **non-absorbing** sub-block is found using the **fundamental matrix** $N = (I - Q)^{-1}$ where $Q$ is the transient sub-matrix:

$$
Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.2 \\ 0.05 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $(I - Q)$.**

$$
I - Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1-0.7 & -0.2 \\ -0.05 & 1-0.8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & -0.2 \\ -0.05 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the determinant.**

$$
\det(I - Q) = (0.3)(0.2) - (-0.2)(-0.05) = 0.06 - 0.01 = 0.05
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the inverse $(I - Q)^{-1}$.**

$$
(I - Q)^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.05} \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.2 \\ 0.05 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 1 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 9 — Read the fundamental matrix $N = (I - Q)^{-1}$.**

$$
N = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 1 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 10 — Interpret $N$ economically.**

- $N_{11} = 4$: a current Worker is expected to spend *4 years* as a Worker before quitting or being promoted.
- $N_{12} = 4$: a current Worker is expected to spend *4 years* as a Supervisor.
- $N_{21} = 1$: a current Supervisor is expected to spend *1 year* as a Worker (demotion/rotation).
- $N_{22} = 6$: a current Supervisor is expected to spend *6 years* as a Supervisor.

**Step 11 — Convert into steady-state proportions.**

Proportion of time spent in state $i$ = $\dfrac{\text{row total of } i}{\text{grand total of all entries}}$.

Row totals: $N_{1\cdot} = 8$, $N_{2\cdot} = 7$, Grand total $= 15$.

$$
\pi_1 = \frac{8}{15} \approx 0.533, \quad \pi_2 = \frac{7}{15} \approx 0.467
$$

**Step 12 — Translate to absolute headcount for a 100-person stable workforce.**

$$
\text{Workers} = 100 \times \frac{8}{15} \approx 53, \quad \text{Supervisors} = 100 \times \frac{7}{15} \approx 47
$$

> [!NOTE] KTU 14-mark interpretation: "Interpret $N_{ij}$ in business language and derive long-run composition" — this is the exact phrasing pattern examiners use.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Recruitment Source Comparison

> [!TIP] Use this table verbatim in a 14-mark answer when the question says "Compare internal and external sources of recruitment."

| Parameter | Internal Sources | External Sources |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Low (no advertising cost) | High (ads, agency fees, campus drives) |
| **Time** | Fast (transfer/promotion in days) | Slow (45–90 days typical) |
| **Quality of Hire** | Known; lower risk | Unknown; higher risk but more diverse |
| **Morale of Existing Staff** | Boosts (career path visible) | Demotivates if overused |
| **Fresh Talent** | Limited | Abundant |
| **Training Required** | Minimal | Significant induction needed |
| **Innovation Potential** | Moderate | High (outside-in perspective) |
| **Suits Entrepreneurial Startup?** | Phase 1 (founding team) | Phase 2 (scaling) — combined with ESOPs |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Return on Investment of a Training Program (Phillips Model)

**Problem:** *"A firm spent ₹2,00,000 on a 4-week leadership program for 25 managers. Annual performance gains attributable to the program are valued at ₹5,50,000. Annual payroll of these 25 managers is ₹75,00,000. Compute the ROI of the program."*

**Step 1 — Recall the Phillips 5-level ROI formula.**

$$
ROI_{\%} = \frac{(\text{Net Program Benefits}) - (\text{Program Costs})}{\text{Program Costs}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Identify benefits.**

- Performance gain: ₹5,50,000
- (Monetary benefit = performance gain in this simplified problem)

**Step 3 — Identify costs.**

- Program cost: ₹2,00,000

**Step 4 — Compute Net Program Benefits.**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = 5{,}50{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Compute ROI.**

$$
ROI_{\%} = \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 175\%
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Utility / Adjusted formula (the more rigorous Phillips form).**

$$
ROI_{\%} = \frac{(\text{Benefits} \times \text{Confidence Adjustment}) - \text{Costs}}{\text{Costs}} \times 100
$$

If the company uses a 90 % confidence adjustment:

$$
ROI_{\%} = \frac{(5{,}50{,}000 \times 0.90) - 2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

**Step 7 — Compute step-by-step.**

$$
5{,}50{,}000 \times 0.90 = 4{,}95{,}000
$$

$$
4{,}95{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}95{,}000
$$

$$
ROI_{\%} = \frac{2{,}95{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 147.5\%
$$

**Step 8 — KTU examiner's expected commentary.**

> "An ROI of 147.5 % indicates that for every ₹1 invested, the firm recovers ₹1.475 in monetized benefits — a strong case for scaling the program to the next 25 managers."

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Motivation Theories (Exhaustive KTU Map)

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Module 4 lists motivation as a *core* sub-topic. Memorize this matrix; it carries 7 marks in a typical 14-mark question.

| Theory | Proposer | Key Idea | Implication for Personnel Manager | Entrepreneurial Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs** | Abraham Maslow (1943) | 5-tier pyramid: Physiological → Safety → Social → Esteem → Self-Actualization | Layered rewards: pay, security, belonging, recognition, growth | Give co-founders autonomy & mission; pay junior staff competitively |
| **Two-Factor Theory** | Frederick Herzberg (1959) | Hygiene factors (pay, policy) prevent dissatisfaction; Motivators (achievement, growth) drive satisfaction | Hygiene ≠ Motivation. Don't mistake good pay for "engagement" | Stock options = motivator; ping-pong table ≠ motivator |
| **Theory X and Theory Y** | Douglas McGregor (1960) | Theory X: people dislike work; Theory Y: people seek responsibility | Choose management style based on workforce maturity | Founder must evolve from X (early stage) to Y (scaling) |
| **Expectancy Theory** | Victor Vroom (1964) | Motivation = Expectancy × Instrumentality × Valence | Link performance → reward visibly & fairly | Offer clear ESOP-based upside |
| **Equity Theory** | J. Stacy Adams (1963) | People compare their input/output ratio with peers | Ensure internal & external pay equity | Transparent comp bands prevent co-founder conflict |
| **Reinforcement Theory** | B.F. Skinner | Behavior shaped by consequences | Use positive reinforcement over punishment | Celebrate small innovation wins publicly |
| **McClelland's Needs Theory** | David McClelland | Need for Achievement, Affiliation, Power | Tailor roles: high-nAch → entrepreneurial tasks | Recruit sales-hungry co-founders from achievement backgrounds |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Leadership Styles (Hersey–Blanchard + KTU Extensions)

| Style | Decision-Making | When to Use | Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Telling (S1)** | High task, low relationship | New, unskilled recruits | Demotivates experienced staff |
| **Selling (S2)** | High task, high relationship | Willing but unskilled | Time-intensive |
| **Participating (S3)** | Low task, high relationship | Skilled but insecure | Slow decisions |
| **Delegating (S4)** | Low task, low relationship | Mature, skilled, motivated | Risk of drift |
| **Transformational** *(KTU extension)* | Inspires beyond self-interest | Innovation-driven teams | Risk of founder burnout |
| **Servant Leadership** *(KTU extension)* | Serves the team first | Knowledge-worker startups | May appear "weak" in crises |
| **Entrepreneurial Leadership** *(KTU extension)* | Risk-taking, vision-led | Early-stage ventures | May centralize decision-making |
